Moving through the per-chapter audit now — the higher direct image chapter is straightforward with just one definition marked complete, the acyclic resolution chapter has the full horseshoe lemma chain and is well-documented, and now I'm diving into the main Čech higher direct image chapter to check the specific focus regions.

For the 02KG residual chain on the critical path, I need to verify that `lem:affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh` has faithful statements with detailed proofs and complete `\uses{}` declarations without leftover infrastructure or missing `\leanok` markers, then confirm the residual lemma is properly pinned to the change-of-ring recipe, and finally audit the supporting lemmas around localization and comparison isomorphisms.

The main lemma is formalized as `affine_cech_vanishing_qcoh_of_tildeVanishing` in an axiom-clean way, transporting the vanishing property from the tilde construction through the specified dependencies. The `\uses{}` references check out against the Stacks Project source, and the leandag output confirms there are no broken edges, so all the labels exist in the blueprint. Now I'm examining the `lem:cechCohomology_isZero_of_iso` lemma.

Moving through the next few lemmas: `lem:affine_cover_span_localizationAway` uses a comap argument with clean structure, and `lem:away_comparison_isLocalizedModule` appears to be starting but got cut off. already been formalized in Lean, which suggests the infrastructure is solid even without an explicit proof sketch in the blueprint. Now looking at `lem:affine_cech_vanishing_tilde_subcover`, which references `AlgebraicGeometry.sectionCech_homology_exact_of_localizationAway` and depends on several other lemmas about the Čech module complex and homology exactness.

The proof strategy follows a three-step approach: first establishing exactness on the R_f side by instantiating the module exactness lemma, then transferring degreewise using the localization comparison, and finally deriving the vanishing homology from exactness. Now I'm verifying the second region with `lem:cech_augmented_resolution`, which connects the Čech nerve definition to acyclic affine covers.  The proof uses a stalk-at-prime argument with a contracting homotopy: for each affine open U = Spec(A) and prime p, the tilde isomorphism produces sections in the localization, and choosing an index where f becomes a unit gives the homotopy that contracts to zero, invoking the acyclic affine lemma to conclude exactness.

The statement and proof both reference the same dependencies, but they're missing `lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma`, which is actually used in the proof body. I need to add this to at least the proof's `\uses{}` block. Let me continue checking the rest of the chapter for similar issues.

I've already reviewed the cech nerve definitions, push-pull functor, affine vanishing sections, presheaf machinery, residual chain, and the P5a comparisons section where I found this wire-up problem.   Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of

  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
  \[
    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
  \]
  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).

  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no

{
  "id": "lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma",
  "type": "lemma",
  "title": "Quasi-coherence implies the counit is an isomorphism, via section-localization (01I8)",
  "chapter": "{\v C",
  "statement": "\textit{Source: Stacks Project, Schemes, Tag 01I8, \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine}.}
  Let \(X = \operatorname{Spec} R\) and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent
  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit
  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma} : \widetilde{\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})} \to \mathcal{F}\) is an
  isomorphism. This is the affine structure theorem 01I8, proved here by checking the counit on the
  basis of distinguished opens. It is the unconditional instance that discharges the
  \(\operatorname{IsIso}(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma})\) hypothesis of
  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}.",
  "uses": [
    "lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule",
    "lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib",
    "lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib",
    "lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib",
    "lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib"
  ],
  "lean_name": "AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_quasicoherent, AlgebraicGeometry.isIso_fromTildeΓ_app_basicOpen",
  "proved": false,
  "mathlib_ok": false,
  "proof_tex": "
  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule, lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib,
    lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib, lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib,
    lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}
  The forgetful functor reflects isomorphisms and the distinguished opens \(\{D(f)\}\) form a basis
  of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:forget_reflectsIso_mathlib}), so it suffices to show
  that the component of \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) over each \(D(f)\) is an isomorphism. By
  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_mathlib} that component is the localization lift
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule.lift}(\operatorname{powers} f)\) of the section-restriction map
  \(\rho_f : \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \to \Gamma(D(f), \mathcal{F})\) along the structure-sheaf
  localization \(\widetilde{(-)}.\operatorname{toOpen}\), the latter being an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of \(f\). By the keystone
  Lemma~\ref{lem:qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule}, \(\rho_f\) is itself an
  \(\operatorname{IsLocalizedModule}\) at the powers of \(f\); hence both the source and the target of
  the component are localizations of \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\) at the powers of \(f\), and the lift
  between two such localizations is an isomorphism
  (Lemma~\ref{lem:isLocalizedModule_linearEquiv_mathlib}). Thus
  \(\operatorname{fromTilde\Gamma}\) is an isomorphism on every \(D(f)\), and therefore an
  isomorphism. (Equivalently, this exhibits \(\mathcal{F}\) in the essential image of
  \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the criterion of Lemma~\ref{lem:isIso_fromTildeGamma_iff_mathlib}.)
",
  "lean_source": "instance isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_quasicoherent (F : (Spec R).Modules) [F.IsQuasicoherent] :
    IsIso F.fromTildeΓ := by
  suffices h : IsIso (modulesSpecToSheaf.map F.fromTildeΓ) from
    SpecModulesToSheafFullyFaithful.isIso_of_isIso_map F.fromTildeΓ
  haveI hcd : (inducedFunctor (fun r : R => specBasicOpen r)).IsCoverDense
      (Opens.grothendieckTopology ↥(Spec R)) :=
    TopCat.Opens.coverDense_inducedFunctor PrimeSpectrum.isBasis_basic_opens
  apply Functor.IsCoverDense.iso_of_restrict_iso
    (G := inducedFunctor (fun r : R => specBasicOpen r))
  haveI : ∀ X, IsIso (((inducedFunctor (fun r : R => specBasicOpen r)).op.whiskerLeft
      (modulesSpecToSheaf.map F.fromTildeΓ).hom).app X) :=
    fun X => isIso_fromTildeΓ_app_basicOpen F X.unop
  exact NatIso.isIso_of_isIso_app _

private lemma isIso_fromTildeΓ_app_basicOpen (F : (Spec R).Modules) [F.IsQuasicoherent] (r : R) :
    IsIso ((modulesSpecToSheaf.map F.fromTildeΓ).hom.app
      (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r))) := by
  set c := (modulesSpecToSheaf.map F.fromTildeΓ).hom.app
    (Opposite.op (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)) with hc

  haveI hlf : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers r)
      (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
        (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom := inferInstance

  haveI hlg : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers r)
      ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.map
        (homOfLE (le_top : PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r ≤ ⊤)).op).hom :=
    qcoh_section_isLocalizedModule F r

  have hcomp := Scheme.Modules.toOpen_fromTildeΓ_app F (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)
  have hcomp' : c.hom ∘ₗ
        (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
          (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom
      = ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.map
          (homOfLE (le_top : PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r ≤ ⊤)).op).hom := by
    rw [← ModuleCat.hom_comp]
    exact congrArg ModuleCat.Hom.hom hcomp
  haveI hcl : IsLocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers r)
      (c.hom ∘ₗ
        (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
          (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom) := by
    rw [hcomp']; exact hlg

  set e := IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv (Submonoid.powers r)
    (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
      (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom
    (c.hom ∘ₗ (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
      (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom) with he
  have hce : c.hom = e.toLinearMap := by
    apply IsLocalizedModule.ext (Submonoid.powers r)
      (tilde.toOpen ((modulesSpecToSheaf.obj F).presheaf.obj (.op ⊤))
        (PrimeSpectrum.basicOpen r)).hom hcl.map_units
    ext x
    simp only [he, LinearMap.comp_apply, LinearEquiv.coe_coe, IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv_apply]
  rw [ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
  change Function.Bijective ⇑c.hom
  rw [hce]
  exact e.bijective",

96	  ### Strategy / multi-route handling
97	
98	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and I report that one
99	    or more routes have no blueprint coverage, dispatch the catalog's
100	    blueprint-writing subagent (one call per missing-coverage route)
101	    in the same iteration. Do not let provers begin work on a route
102	    until its blueprint coverage is in place.
103	  - If I flag a definition that may require a strategy modification,
104	    treat that as a STRATEGY.md update task before any further Lean work
105	    — provers cannot be dispatched until the strategy update is reflected
106	    in the blueprint.
107	
108	  ### What "deferred" means in practice
109	
110	  Deferring a prover round is the correct, intentional action — not a
111	  failure. The 1-iter latency cost of waiting for a writer is small
112	  compared to the cost of a prover formalizing a broken blueprint and
113	  the work being thrown away. Log the deferral cleanly in plan.md and
114	  move on; the next iter's mandatory me-dispatch will green-light F.
115	---
116	
117	# Blueprint Reviewer
118	
119	You read the **entire blueprint** plus a context bundle from the plan agent and produce a per-chapter checklist + a set of cross-cutting summaries. You are **read-only on project source** and the blueprint — your only writable target is your own report under `task_results/`.
120	
121	You are **mandatory in the plan phase**: the plan agent dispatches you every iteration before writing prover objectives. Your output is the plan agent's primary window into blueprint health, so the plan agent doesn't have to read every chapter itself.
122	
123	## Your Job
124	
125	The plan agent gives you a directive containing the current strategy snapshot, the references you should treat as authoritative, and any specific concerns. You then audit:
126	
127	- **Completeness** — for each chapter, are the definitions / theorems the strategy says the project needs actually present? Is the proof sketch detailed enough for a prover to formalize without guessing? Are the cross-references (`\uses{...}`) accurate?
128	- **Correctness** — does any definition contradict its references? Does any proof sketch contain a step that doesn't follow? Does any `\lean{...}` hint name a declaration that doesn't exist or has the wrong signature?
129	- **Dependency-graph integrity (use `leandag`)** — the `\uses{}` edges must reflect the real mathematical dependencies. `leandag` computes the actual DAG, so use it instead of eyeballing:
130	  - **Broken edges** — `leandag build --json` reports `unknown_uses` (a `\uses{}` pointing at a label no declaration defines). Every one is a must-fix.
131	  - **Missing edges** — read each proof: if it relies on a lemma/definition that its `\uses{}` does not list, the edge is missing. These don't show up as "broken"; you find them by reading the math against the declared `\uses{}`. Missing edges make the loop dispatch provers out of order, so flag them as correctness findings.
132	  - **Isolated declarations** — `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated --type theorem`, and the `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint` counts in `leandag stats`) lists nodes with **no `\uses{}` out and nothing using them**. An isolated *blueprint* node is almost always a *symptom of a missing edge*, not dead weight — so for each one decide and record a disposition (see below). Isolated `lean_aux` nodes are uncovered Lean helpers, a separate "needs a blueprint entry" signal, not removal candidates.
133	- **Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)** — it is good practice for a chapter that relies on a Mathlib-provided result to state it as an explicit anchor block marked `\mathlibok` (with `\lean{}` naming the real Mathlib declaration), rather than a bare `\uses{}` to nothing. Treat such anchors as *valid and done* — never flag them as ∞ holes or `remove` candidates. But **audit each `\mathlibok` claim for faithfulness**, because a wrong one makes the loop skip a real gap: does Mathlib actually contain the named `\lean{}` declaration, and does the stated form match it? Verify against Lean (`archon-lean-lsp`) when in doubt. A `\mathlibok` on a statement Mathlib does not provide — or whose form is stronger/different than Mathlib's — is a hard fail (treat like a fabricated citation). Also: where a broken `\uses{}` points at what is really a Mathlib result, the right `wire-up` is "add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it," not "invent a project lemma."
134	- **Lean target formulation quality** — for each `\lean{...}` hint, is the named theorem/definition a *useful* target for the prover? Vague or under-specified hints lead to wrong formalizations; surface those.
135	- **Rendering integrity (run `archon blueprint-doctor`)** — the blueprint must *render*: as the compiled leanblueprint site and in the dashboard. The doctor lints this deterministically — run `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once and triage every `malformed_refs` finding into your report:
136	  - **`undefined-macro`** — a chapter uses `\foo` that no `macros/*.tex` or chapter-local `\providecommand` defines: it renders as raw TeX everywhere. Recommend the definition (usually obvious from context: `\Z` → `\mathbb{Z}`, `\fppf` → `\mathrm{fppf}`); when the intent is genuinely unclear (you cannot tell what the writer meant), flag it as a correctness finding for the chapter's writer instead of guessing.
137	  - **`math-delim`** — interleaved `$ … \( … \) … $` delimiters that shred formulas mid-sentence; per-line locations are in the finding. Must-fix, writer-directive material.
138	  - **`literal-ref` / `bare-label`** — placeholder "REF" tokens and raw label ids in prose; each needs a `\cref{}` or the human-readable number.
139	  Group these per chapter in your findings so the dispatcher can scope one writer per damaged chapter.
140	- **Multi-route coverage** — if the strategy lists multiple viable routes (alternative proof approaches, alternative definitions), is each route represented in the blueprint? Routes the strategy mentions but the blueprint does not cover are red flags.
141	- **Unstarted phases** — for each phase in the strategy that has no blueprint chapter at all, produce a concrete chapter outline (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" below). This is a proactive planning function, not just a gap report.
142	- **Citation discipline** — for every definition / theorem / lemma block that derives from external reference material, audit all four elements:
143	  1. **`% SOURCE:` pointer with local-file parenthetical.** Format must be `% SOURCE: <pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. Verify the named local file EXISTS under `references/`. A `% SOURCE:` with no `(read from …)` parenthetical, or with a parenthetical naming a file that doesn't exist on disk, is a hard fail — the writer fabricated the citation.
144	  2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` verbatim text** for definitions / theorems / lemmas. Audit dimensions:
145	     - **Original language**: the quote must be in the source's original language. A quote in English when the source is Bourbaki / EGA (French) signals translation, which is not allowed — flag it.
146	     - **Original notation**: the quote must use the source's notation, even when it differs from the project's. If the project writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ everywhere but the `% SOURCE QUOTE:` also writes $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$ when the source is Hartshorne (who writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$), the quote was rewritten — flag it.
147	     - **Verbatim, every word**: a quote that reads like a paraphrase ("essentially says that …", "the source states …") rather than direct copy is a hard fail. The whole point of the verbatim is anti-hallucination; paraphrased quotes are exactly the failure mode.
148	  3. **`% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`** immediately before the `\begin{proof}` environment, when the block has a proof and the proof derives from the source. Same verbatim rules as `% SOURCE QUOTE:`. Missing `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` on a theorem whose proof clearly comes from the cited source is a citation-discipline finding. (Archon-original proofs of external statements are allowed — flag only when the proof prose itself reads as a translation of an obvious source proof.)
149	  4. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}`** line as the first line of the block's prose. Missing → flag.
150	
151	  Cross-check: the visible `\textit{Source: ...}` pointer must match the `% SOURCE:` pointer. Drift between them signals copy-paste error or hallucination.
152	
153	  Spot-check against `## References consulted` in the corresponding writer's report (when available in `task_results/`): every distinct `references/<file>.md` named in `% SOURCE:` parentheticals across the chapter should appear in that list. A `% SOURCE: ... (read from references/X.md)` where the writer's "References consulted" list does NOT mention `references/X.md` means the writer cited a file they did not actually open this session — fabrication.
154	
155	  **Archon-original / project-bespoke** results (no external source) omit the source lines entirely — do not falsely flag those. The signal that a block is Archon-original: the directive that produced it didn't name an external source, or the chapter prose explicitly characterizes it as new (e.g. "This is the technical heart of our argument"). When in doubt, ask the plan agent in "Notes for Plan Agent" rather than flagging.
156	
157	You audit the blueprint **against the context the plan agent gave you**, not against your own opinions about how the math should be set up. But you are critical of weak prose — under-specified blueprints fail provers and are not safe to merge.
158	
159	`/references/summary.md` lists the project's reference materials. The planner writes the blueprints, while its knowledge might be enough to write some parts of the blueprint, some parts may be subject to hallucination and require reference material. If you believe a reference is required to write mathematically correct and complete blueprint chapters, you should mention it in your report so that the planner can retrieve it for the writer.
160	
161	## Always read everything
162	
163	**Read every chapter under `blueprint/src/chapters/`**, no exceptions, regardless of project size. The cross-chapter view is the entire reason for running me. If the directive contains a "scope" hint, treat it as a focus suggestion (which chapters need extra attention), not as a permission to skip reading.
164	
165	## Unstarted-phase proposals
166	
167	This is a proactive function — you produce it in addition to the per-chapter audit, not instead of it.
168	
169	After completing the per-chapter checklist, cross-reference the strategy snapshot's `## Phases & estimations` table against the set of chapters you just read. (That table lists only *remaining* phases — finished ones live in `## Completed` and need no proposal.) For each remaining phase row whose content has **zero blueprint coverage** (no chapter exists, or the only existing chapter is a stub with fewer than ~3 meaningful declaration blocks), produce a **chapter outline proposal**.
170	
171	A chapter outline proposal is not a flag or a complaint — it is a concrete, actionable seed that the plan agent can hand directly to a blueprint-writing subagent. It must contain enough mathematical detail that a writer receiving it as a directive can produce a complete chapter without further research by the plan agent.
172	
173	The proposal answers:
174	
175	1. **Which Lean file(s) will this chapter cover?** Name the expected file path(s) and whether this should be a consolidated chapter (one chapter covering multiple files via `% archon:covers`) or a 1:1 chapter.
176	
177	2. **What are the declaration blocks the chapter needs?** For each: the block type (definition / lemma / theorem / proposition), a proposed `\label`, a one-sentence description of the mathematical content, the expected `\lean{...}` hint (even if speculative — tag it `[expected]`), and the likely reference source (from `references/summary.md` or a named standard reference). Do not write the full prose — that is the writer's job — but give enough that the writer knows exactly what to write.
178	
179	3. **What are the internal dependencies?** Which declarations within this chapter use others (the `\uses{...}` graph in skeletal form). Identifying this upfront prevents the writer from writing declarations in an order that creates circular `\uses` references.
180	
181	4. **What is the proof strategy for the chapter's main theorem(s)?** Two to four sentences naming the key steps, the supporting lemmas, and any Mathlib infrastructure the proofs will depend on. This is the piece most likely to require a reference; if you can identify the relevant section of a source in `references/summary.md`, name it.
182	
183	5. **What references should the blueprint-writer read first?** List the specific local files under `references/` that are most relevant, and the sections within them. If no local file covers the material, flag it as a retrieval need so the plan agent can dispatch a reference-retriever before the writer.
184	
185	6. **Are there subphase choices the outline exposes?** If the chapter's material admits multiple natural decompositions (e.g. the main theorem could be approached via method A or method B, or the definition could be bundled or unbundled), name them explicitly. The plan agent cannot make an informed subphase choice without seeing what the choices are — surfacing them here is the primary value of writing the blueprint early rather than late.
186	
187	**Format for each proposal** (one block per unstarted phase):
188	
189	```
190	### Proposed chapter: <expected filename, e.g. `blueprint/src/chapters/Foo_Bar.tex`>
191	
192	**Covers**: `Foo/Bar.lean` (+ any sibling files if consolidated)
193	**Strategy phase**: <phase name from STRATEGY.md>
194	**Why now**: <one sentence on why writing this chapter this iter, before any prover work, is the right move — what ambiguity it resolves, what parallelism it enables>
195	
196	**Key declarations** (in dependency order):
197	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <reference + section, or "no source identified — Archon-original">
198	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
199	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
200	...
201	
202	**`\uses` skeleton**:
203	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
204	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
205	
206	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
207	
208	**References for writer**:
209	- `references/<slug>.md` → `<file>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
210	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name> — no local file exists yet">
211	
212	**Subphase choices exposed**:
213	- Choice A vs Choice B: <one sentence describing each option and the trade-off>. Recommendation: <your read on which is better given the strategy, or "unclear — plan agent should decide">
214	- (omit this field if the chapter has a clear single decomposition)
215	```
216	
217	**Tone and completeness.** The proposal should be aggressive about detail — it is cheaper to write a thorough proposal now than to have the writer ask for clarification later. If the phase is large enough that one chapter cannot cover it, say so and propose the split (two or more chapter outlines, each covering a natural sub-unit).
218	
219	**Do not propose chapters for phases that already have adequate coverage.** A phase with ≥3 meaningful declaration blocks across its chapters is covered; do not reproduce a proposal for it. The signal for "adequate" is the same as for the per-chapter checklist: `complete: true` (or `partial` with only minor gaps) on the phase's relevant chapters.
220	
221	**Proposals are must-act-this-iter.** They land in `## Must-fix-this-iter` under the label `unstarted-phase proposal` alongside the hard-gate findings. The plan agent must either dispatch a blueprint-writer for each proposed chapter or record an explicit one-line deferral rationale in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`. Silently ignoring the proposals is the same failure mode as silently ignoring a CHURNING verdict.
222	
223	## Directive Format
224	
225	```markdown
226	# Blueprint Reviewer Directive
227	
228	## Slug
229	<slug>
230	
231	## Strategy snapshot
232	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted: the project's end-state and the chapters that bear on it. Tells you what each chapter MUST contain to support the strategy. MUST include the full `## Phases & estimations` table so you can identify unstarted phases.>
233	
234	## Routes
235	<if the strategy has more than one viable route, list each route here with one line on what's distinctive about it and which chapters / definitions are exclusive to that route. If only one route, write "single route".>
236	
237	## References
238	- <path/to/reference.md>: <topic + which chapters depend on it>
239	- <arxiv ID>: <topic>
240	
241	## Focus areas (optional)
242	<chapters or theorems the plan agent wants extra attention on this iter — bias for thoroughness here, do not skip the others>
243	
244	## Known issues
245	<things the plan agent already knows and doesn't want re-reported>
246	```
247	
248	## What you do
249	
250	1. **Read your directive completely.** Extract the `## Phases & estimations` table — you will need it for the unstarted-phase proposals.
251	2. **List every chapter** under `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`.
252	3. **For each chapter** (no exceptions, no scope shortcuts):
253	   - Read the entire chapter.
254	   - Check every declaration block against the strategy snapshot.
255	   - For each proof block: are steps sound? Are `\uses{...}` cross-refs real labels? Is detail adequate for a prover?
256	   - For each `\lean{...}`: is the named target well-formulated? (You may verify existence using the `archon-lean-lsp` MCP tools — read-only.)
257	4. **Compute completeness/correctness verdicts** per chapter (`true | partial | false`).
258	5. **Note cross-chapter inconsistencies** as you find them (e.g. `def X` in chapter A doesn't match the use of `X` in chapter B). These go in the "Cross-chapter notes" section.
259	6. **Audit the dependency graph with `leandag`.** Run `leandag build --json` (broken/unknown `\uses{}`, `unmatched_lean`, isolated count) and `leandag show isolated` once for the whole blueprint. Then triage each isolated **blueprint** node into exactly one disposition and record it:
260	   - **wire-up** — it *should* connect to the graph but an edge is missing or mislabeled. Name the `\uses{}` that should be added (and on which side). This is the common case and a correctness finding — recommend a writer fix, do NOT recommend removal.
261	   - **remove** — it is genuinely orphaned scaffolding: nothing in the goal's proof closure needs it, it isn't the goal, and it isn't a `\mathlibok`/reference anchor. Only then is removal the right call. Name the chapter + label so the plan agent can authorize a writer to delete it.
262	   - **keep** — it is intentionally standalone (the goal theorem with nothing above it, a deliberately isolated `\mathlibok` reference, etc.). Say why and move on.
263	   You **flag and recommend**; you never edit. The plan agent turns a `wire-up`/`remove` disposition into a blueprint-writer directive.
264	7. **Run the rendering lint.** `archon blueprint-doctor --json` once for the whole blueprint; triage every `malformed_refs` finding (`undefined-macro`, `math-delim`, `literal-ref`, `bare-label`) per the "Rendering integrity" bullet above, grouped per chapter.
265	8. **Check multi-route coverage**: for each route listed in the directive's `## Routes`, identify which chapters cover it. Flag any route that has zero or insufficient blueprint coverage.
266	9. **Cross-reference phases against chapters**: for each row in the strategy's `## Phases & estimations` table, determine whether adequate blueprint coverage exists. For every phase with no or stub coverage, produce a chapter outline proposal (see "Unstarted-phase proposals" above).
267	10. **Produce three top-level summaries** (see report format) — these are what the plan agent acts on first.
268	
269	You may also use:
270	- `archon-lean-lsp`: read-only Lean LSP operations (search, hover, diagnostics) to verify `\lean{...}` references.
271	- **`archon blueprint-doctor [--json]`** — the deterministic rendering/structure lint (orphan chapters, broken refs, literal-REF, interleaved math delimiters, bare labels, undefined macros, covers problems). Read-only; run it once per review and triage its `malformed_refs` (see "Rendering integrity" above).
272	- **`leandag`** — your read-only window into the real dependency DAG. Run it freely: it never touches project source or the blueprint. (`leandag build` refreshes the derived `.leandag/` cache, which is regenerated deterministically from the current tree — that is a tool artifact, not a project edit, so it does not violate your read-only rule.)
273	  - `leandag build --json` — the structured build report: `unknown_uses` (broken `\uses{}`), `unmatched_lean` (`\lean{}` pointing nowhere), `conflicts`, and `summary.isolated`.
274	  - `leandag stats` — counts including `isolated` / `isolated_blueprint`.
275	  - `leandag show isolated` (or `leandag query --isolated [--type theorem] [--chapter <c>]`) — the isolated declarations, which you triage per node (below).
276	  - `leandag show gaps` — blueprint nodes missing a `\lean{}`.
277	
278	You do **not** modify any project file, including the blueprint. Even if you spot a clear fix, you report it; the plan agent decides what to do next iter.
279	
280	## Report format
281	
282	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/blueprint-reviewer-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
283	
284	**Omit-empty rule.** Required sections: `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Per-chapter` (the HARD GATE depends on it), `## Severity summary`, `Overall verdict`. **Everything else is optional and must be omitted when empty.**
285	
286	Concretely:
287	
288	- `### Incomplete parts`, `### Proofs lacking detail`, `### Lean difficulty quality`, `### Multi-route coverage`, `### Citation discipline`, `### Dependency & isolation findings` under `## Top-level summaries`: omit each sub-section whose finding list is empty. If all six are empty, omit `## Top-level summaries` entirely — the per-chapter table already encodes "everything is fine".
289	- `## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals`: omit only when every phase in the strategy has adequate blueprint coverage. When present, this section is never empty — each proposal block is a concrete outline, not a one-line flag.
290	- `## Cross-chapter notes`: omit when no cross-chapter findings exist (the most common case on a clean blueprint).
291	- `## Strategy-modifying findings (if any)`: omit entirely when none. Do NOT write a section with "None" inside; the absence of the section IS the signal.
292	- `## Per-chapter` blocks for clean chapters: when a chapter is `complete: true`, `correct: true`, and `notes` is empty, render it as a single compact line — `### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex — complete + correct, no notes.` — NOT a multi-line block with `notes: -` filler. Reserve full multi-line blocks for chapters that have actual findings.
293	- `## Severity summary`: when there are zero must-fix-this-iter and zero soon-severity items, render as `Severity summary: HARD GATE CLEARS — no findings.` and skip the per-tier breakdown.
294	
295	Filling templates with hollow "(none)" / "no drift" / "OK" content per chapter is the bloat the report should not produce. A clean 11-chapter audit should be a few hundred lines, not 16K tokens.
296	
297	```markdown
298	# Blueprint Review Report
299	
300	## Slug
301	<slug>
302	
303	## Iteration
304	<NNN>
305	
306	## Top-level summaries
307	
308	### Incomplete parts
309	<bullets naming chapter + which definition/theorem/proof is missing or shallow. Sorted by severity.>
310	- `Foo.tex`: definition of `<name>` is missing entirely.
311	- `Bar.tex`: theorem `\thm:baz` has a one-sentence "proof" — not enough for a prover to formalize.
312	
313	### Proofs lacking detail
314	<bullets naming chapter + theorem label + what's vague. Distinct from "incomplete" — the proof exists but isn't enough.>
315	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: jumps from "X holds" to "therefore Y" without naming the lemma used.
316	
317	### Lean difficulty quality
318	<bullets naming `\lean{<name>}` hints whose target is poorly formulated for a prover (too vague, ambiguous, or pointing at something that would lead to a bad type).>
319	- `Foo.tex` / `\lean{Foo.frobnicate}`: signature unclear from prose; prover has no way to infer return type.
320	
321	### Multi-route coverage
322	<one block per route mentioned in directive's Routes. PASS / PARTIAL / MISSING + which chapters cover it.>
323	- Route "via cohomology": PARTIAL — covered in `Cohomology.tex` and `RR.tex` but `Bridge.tex` (which links them) is empty.
324	- Route "via direct computation": MISSING — strategy mentions this as an alternative but no chapter discusses it.
325	
326	### Citation discipline
327	<bullets naming chapter + label + which citation element is missing or suspect. Omit when zero findings.>
328	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:smooth_criterion`: `% SOURCE:` line has no `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical — writer did not name the local file. Likely fabrication.
329	- `Bar.tex` / `\def:scheme`: `% SOURCE:` claims `(read from references/hartshorne-II-1.md)` but that file does not exist on disk. Fabrication; writer must dispatch a retriever or remove the citation.
330	
331	### Dependency & isolation findings
332	<from the leandag audit. Each line: chapter / label — finding + disposition the plan agent should hand a writer. Omit when zero findings.>
333	- `Bar.tex` / `\thm:foo`: `\uses{}` is missing `lem:bar` (the proof's "by the gluing lemma" step depends on it). **wire-up** — add `\uses{lem:bar}`.
334	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:foo` → `\lem:gone`: broken `\uses{}` (no declaration defines `lem:gone`). **fix** — correct the label or add the missing lemma.
335	- `Aux.tex` / `\lem:orphan`: isolated (no `\uses{}` out, nothing uses it), not the goal, not `\mathlibok`, nothing in the goal closure needs it. **remove** — authorize a writer to delete it from `Aux.tex`.
336	- `Main.tex` / `\thm:goal`: isolated but it is the project goal. **keep**.
337	- `Foo.tex` / `\thm:foo` → `\lem:hensel`: broken `\uses{}`, and `lem:hensel` is a Mathlib result. **wire-up** — add a `\mathlibok` anchor for it (state it, `\lean{Mathlib...}`), not a project lemma.
338	- `Bar.tex` / `\lem:claimed_mathlib`: marked `\mathlibok` but Mathlib has no such declaration / the stated form is stronger than Mathlib's. **fix** — hard fail; the loop would skip a real gap. Remove the marker and treat as a result to prove, or correct the statement to match Mathlib.
339	
340	## Unstarted-phase blueprint proposals
341	
342	<One proposal block per strategy phase with no or stub blueprint coverage. Each block is a complete chapter outline as specified in "Unstarted-phase proposals" above — not a one-line flag. Omit this section only when every phase has adequate coverage.>
343	
344	### Proposed chapter: `blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`
345

347	**Strategy phase**: <phase name>
348	**Why now**: <one sentence>
349

351	1. `\definition` `\label{def:foo}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.foo}` [expected]. Source: <...>
352	2. `\lemma` `\label{lem:bar}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.bar}` [expected]. Source: <...>
353	3. `\theorem` `\label{thm:baz}` — <one sentence>. `\lean{Foo.baz}` [expected]. Source: <...>
354	
355	**`\uses` skeleton**:
356	- `thm:baz` uses `lem:bar`, `def:foo`
357	- `lem:bar` uses `def:foo`
358	
359	**Main theorem proof strategy**: <2–4 sentences>
360	
361	**References for writer**:
362	- `references/<slug>.<ext>`, §<section> — <why relevant>
363	- <or: "retrieval needed: <source name>">
364

366	- <choice description and trade-off, or omit field if single decomposition>
367	
368	## Per-chapter
369	
370	### blueprint/src/chapters/Foo.tex
371	- **complete**: true | partial | false
372	- **correct**: true | partial | false
373	- **notes**:
374	  - <missing | wrong | observation> — <one line>
375	  - ...
376	
377	### blueprint/src/chapters/Bar.tex — complete + correct, no notes.
378	
379	(Cover every chapter. Chapters with findings get the full multi-line
380	block. Chapters that are `complete: true`, `correct: true`, with
381	no notes worth surfacing get the compact one-liner.)
382

1	\chapter{{\v C}ech computation of higher direct images $R^i f_*$ (unconditional)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechHigherDirectImage.lean
4	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechAcyclic.lean
5	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/PresheafCech.lean
6	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FreePresheafComplex.lean
7	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechBridge.lean
8	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImagePresheaf.lean
9	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AbsoluteCohomology.lean
10	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/CechToCohomology.lean
11	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AffineSerreVanishing.lean
12	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/QcohTildeSections.lean
13	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/QcohRestrictBasicOpen.lean
14	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
15	% quasi-coherent sheaves and \S Quasi-coherence of higher direct images
16	% (Lemmas \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial},
17	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherent-affine-cohomology-zero},
18	% \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent},
19	% \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}) (read from
20	% references/stacks-coherent.tex).
21	
22	\section{Setup and motivation}
23	
24	This chapter constructs the higher derived direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) for
25	\(i \geq 1\) \emph{without appealing to injective resolutions in the category of
26	sheaves of modules}. The companion development of
27	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage} defines \(R^i f_*\) as a right
28	derived functor via injective resolutions, which requires the ambient category of
29	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to have enough injectives. The {\v C}ech approach
30	developed here sidesteps this requirement:
31	it computes \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) as the cohomology of an explicit complex built
32	from the pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the finite intersections of an affine
33	open cover, and so produces an \emph{unconditional} construction of \(R^i f_*\) for
34	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{F}\) and separated quasi-compact \(f\).
35	
36	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
37	separated (so that all finite intersections of an affine open cover of \(X\) are
38	again affine), and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. We
39	write \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) for the category of quasi-coherent
40	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. A base change of \(f\) along a morphism \(g : S' \to S\)
41	is recorded by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
42	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
43	\[
44	  \begin{array}{ccc}
45	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
46	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
47	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
48	  \end{array}
49	\]
50	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), \(g' : X' \to X\) is the first
51	projection, and \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the pullback of
52	\(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
53	
54	\section{The {\v C}ech nerve and {\v C}ech complex of an affine cover}
55	
56	\begin{definition}
57	
58	\leanok
59	[{\v C}ech nerve of an affine open cover]
60	  \label{def:cech_nerve}
61	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechNerve}
62	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor, def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
63	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
64	  % quasi-coherent sheaves, opening paragraph and Lemma
65	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial}
66	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L36--67).
67	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $X$ be a scheme.
68	  % Let $U \subset X$ be an affine open.
69	  % Recall that a {\it standard open covering} of $U$ is a covering
70	  % of the form $\mathcal{U} : U = \bigcup_{i = 1}^n D(f_i)$
71	  % where $f_1, \ldots, f_n \in \Gamma(U, \mathcal{O}_X)$ generate
72	  % the unit ideal, see
73	  % Schemes, Definition \ref{schemes-definition-standard-covering}."
74	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S{\v C}ech cohomology of
75	  quasi-coherent sheaves.}
76	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let
77	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite open cover of \(X\) by
78	  affine opens, with index set \(I\). For a finite nonempty tuple
79	  \(i_0, \ldots, i_p \in I\) write
80	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = U_{i_0} \cap \cdots \cap U_{i_p}\) for the corresponding
81	  intersection. The \emph{{\v C}ech nerve} of \(\mathcal{U}\) is the augmented
82	  \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\) whose object in
83	  cosimplicial degree \(p\) is the direct image, along the open immersions
84	  \(j_{i_0 \ldots i_p} : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\), of the restriction
85	  of \(\mathcal{O}_X\) (or of any chosen quasi-coherent sheaf) to the
86	  \((p+1)\)-fold intersections,
87	  \[
88	    [p] \;\longmapsto\;
89	      \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
90	        (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_* \,\bigl(\,\cdot\,|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
91	  \]
92	  with cofaces given by the restriction maps that omit one index and
93	  codegeneracies by the maps that repeat one index; the augmentation in degree
94	  \(-1\) is the object on all of \(X\). The variance is essential: the geometric
95	  nerve of the cover (the iterated fibre powers of \(\coprod_i U_i\) over \(X\),
96	  introduced in \S\ref{sec:cech_three_part} below) is a \emph{simplicial} object
97	  in \emph{schemes}, but applying the covariant direct-image functor turns it into
98	  a \emph{cosimplicial} object in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); consequently the
99	  associated alternating-coface complex is a \emph{cochain} complex (differentials
100	  raising degree), not a chain complex. When \(X\) is separated each intersection
101	  \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is itself affine. The local affine model is the
102	  \emph{standard open covering} \(U = \bigcup_{i=1}^n D(f_i)\) of an affine open
103	  \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) by basic opens, where \(f_1, \ldots, f_n \in A\)
104	  generate the unit ideal; on such a cover the intersections are again basic opens
105	  \(D(f_{i_0} \cdots f_{i_p})\).
106	\end{definition}
107	
108	\begin{definition}
109	
110	\leanok
111	[{\v C}ech complex of a quasi-coherent sheaf]
112	  \label{def:cech_complex}
113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.CechComplex}
114	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
115	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
116	  % \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof)
117	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L59--77).
118	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Clearly the {\v C}ech complex
119	  % $\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})$
120	  % is identified with the complex
121	  % $$
122	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
123	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
124	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
125	  % \ldots
126	  % $$
127	  % We are asked to show that the extended complex
128	  % \begin{equation}
129	  % \label{equation-extended}
130	  % 0 \to
131	  % M \to
132	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
133	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
134	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
135	  % \ldots
136	  % \end{equation}
137	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
138	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact."
139	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
140	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (standard-cover {\v C}ech
141	  vanishing).}
142	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
143	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with
144	  all intersections \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) affine (e.g.\ \(X\) separated). The
145	  \emph{{\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
146	  is the cochain complex obtained by applying the global-sections functor to the
147	  {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\);
148	  in degree \(p\) it is
149	  \[
150	    \check{\mathcal{C}}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F})
151	      = \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
152	        \mathcal{F}\bigl(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\bigr),
153	  \]
154	  with differential \(d : \check{\mathcal{C}}^p \to \check{\mathcal{C}}^{p+1}\) the
155	  alternating sum \((d s)_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}} = \sum_{j=0}^{p+1} (-1)^j\,
156	  s_{i_0 \ldots \widehat{i_j} \ldots i_{p+1}}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_{p+1}}}\) of the
157	  restriction maps. Over an affine \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) with
158	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U = \widetilde{M}\) and a standard cover by the
159	  \(D(f_i)\), this is the complex
160	  \(\prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \to \cdots\) of
161	  localisations. The \emph{relative {\v C}ech complex} \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet
162	  (\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) for \(f : X \to S\) is the analogous complex in
163	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) obtained by applying \(f_*\) over each finite
164	  intersection instead of global sections; its terms are
165	  \(\prod_{i_0 \ldots i_p} (f|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})_*
166	  \bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr)\), and it is a complex in
167	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) because each \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is affine and the
168	  pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf along a quasi-compact quasi-separated
169	  morphism is quasi-coherent.
170	\end{definition}
171	
172	\subsection{The three-part construction of the relative {\v C}ech complex}
173	\label{sec:cech_three_part}
174	
175	It is convenient to factor the construction of
176	Definitions~\ref{def:cech_nerve}--\ref{def:cech_complex} into three independent
177	pieces. Two of them are purely formal and unconditional; the sole non-trivial
178	mathematical content is isolated in the middle piece.
179	
180	\paragraph{(1) Geometric backbone — the augmented {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow.}
181	Package the affine cover as a single morphism
182	\(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) (the canonical map out of the
183	coproduct induced by the inclusions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\)). The augmented
184	{\v C}ech nerve of this arrow is an augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
185	\(X\): its object in simplicial degree \(p\) is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
186	\[
187	  \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
188	    \;=\;
189	    \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
190	      U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
191	\]
192	with the augmentation the cover map to \(X\). Because the \(U_i\) are open
193	subschemes of \(X\), each fibre product \(U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p}\)
194	is canonically the intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\). This backbone exists
195	\emph{unconditionally}: it uses only that the category of schemes has coproducts
196	and finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the {\v C}ech nerve of an
197	arrow). It carries no sheaf-theoretic data yet — it is the simplicial diagram of
198	spaces over which the construction is indexed.
199	
200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}

1	\chapter{Higher direct images $R^i f_*$ of quasi-coherent sheaves ($i \geq 1$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_HigherDirectImage}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/HigherDirectImage.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, definition of $R^i f_*$ as the
5	% $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex
6	% and references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter records the derived-functor higher direct images \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\)
11	for \(i \geq 1\). It provides the single object that the {\v C}ech computation of
12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison
13	theorem \(\ref{lem:cech_computes_cohomology}\) identifies the cohomology of the
14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.
16	
17	We use the classical cohomology-sheaf formulation (not the derived category).
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	
21	\section{Definition of the higher direct images}
22	
23	\begin{definition}\leanok
24	[Higher direct image]
25	  \label{def:higher_direct_image}
26	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage}
27	  % NOTE (iter-233): the Lean `higherDirectImage` carries an explicit instance
28	  % hypothesis `[HasInjectiveResolutions X.Modules]`, because Mathlib has
29	  % `Abelian X.Modules` but NOT `EnoughInjectives` / `IsGrothendieckAbelian` for
30	  % `SheafOfModules` over a sheaf of rings (the Grothendieck-abelian instance is
31	  % only for `Sheaf J A` with a fixed value category). The definition is therefore
32	  % conditional; the hypothesis can be dropped once a lane proves that instance.
33	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
34	  % `lemma-relative-affine-vanishing' (proof), describing $R^i f_*$ as the
35	  % $i$-th right derived functor of $f_*$ via the sheafified presheaf
36	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$
37	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L188--192).
38	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "According to
39	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}
40	  % the sheaf
41	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F}$ is the sheaf associated to the presheaf
42	  % $V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V), \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)})$."
43	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Quasi-coherence of
44	  higher direct images (and the underlying Cohomology chapter).}
45	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
46	  quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. For \(i \geq 0\) the \emph{\(i\)-th
47	  higher direct image} \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(i\)-th right derived functor
48	  of the pushforward functor \(f_*\) applied to \(\mathcal{F}\). Concretely,
49	  \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) is the \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module obtained as the
50	  sheafification of the presheaf
51	  \[
52	    V \;\longmapsto\; H^i\bigl(f^{-1}(V),\, \mathcal{F}|_{f^{-1}(V)}\bigr),
53	    \qquad V \subset S \text{ open},
54	  \]
55	  where \(H^i(-, -)\) denotes sheaf cohomology. For \(i = 0\) this recovers the
56	  ordinary pushforward \(R^0 f_*\mathcal{F} = f_*\mathcal{F}\).
57	\end{definition}
58

1	\chapter{Acyclic resolutions compute right-derived functors}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_AcyclicResolution}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/AcyclicResolution.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, \S Higher derived functors
5	% (Definition \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157), Lemmas
6	% \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C), \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D),
7	% \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E) and Proposition
8	% \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA))
9	% (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex).
10	
11	\section{Setup and motivation}
12	
13	This chapter isolates the one abstract, scheme-independent piece of homological
14	algebra on which the {\v C}ech computation of the higher direct images rests: an
15	\emph{acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor}. The companion chapter
16	\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} builds, from a separated quasi-compact
17	morphism \(f : X \to S\) and an affine open cover, an explicit complex of
18	\(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules --- the relative {\v C}ech complex
19	\(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) --- whose terms are
20	pushforwards of \(\mathcal{F}\) over the affine intersections. To identify the
21	cohomology of that complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\), one observes that the
22	{\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) by objects on which the higher
23	direct images vanish (the affine acyclicity of
24	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}), and then invokes the present theorem with
25	\(G = f_*\). This single homological lemma thereby replaces \emph{both} the
26	{\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence and the Leray spectral sequence.
27	
28	Throughout, \(\mathcal{A}\) and \(\mathcal{B}\) are abelian categories,
29	\(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives (so that injective resolutions exist and the
30	right-derived functors below are everywhere defined), and
31	\(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) is an additive functor. For \(n \in \mathbb{N}\)
32	we write \(R^n G\) for the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\)
33	(a functor \(\mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\)), constructed from injective resolutions:
34	for an injective resolution \(X \to I^\bullet\) one has
35	\((R^n G)(X) \cong H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr)\)
36	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} below). When in addition \(G\) is
37	left exact --- equivalently, \(G\) preserves finite limits, which holds for any \(G\)
38	that is a right adjoint, such as a pushforward \(f_*\) --- one has \(R^0 G \cong G\)
39	(Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}).
40	
41	\section{Known results}
42	
43	The following three well-known results underpin the injective-resolution
44	construction of \(R^n G\) and are the foundational inputs to the argument.
45	
46	\begin{lemma}[Right-derived functor via an injective resolution]
47	  \label{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
48	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.isoRightDerivedObj}
49	  \mathlibok
50	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
51	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives,
52	  \(\mathcal{B}\) abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) additive. For an
53	  object \(X\) of \(\mathcal{A}\), a choice of injective resolution
54	  \(I\) of \(X\) (an exact complex \(0 \to X \to I^0 \to I^1 \to \cdots\) with each
55	  \(I^n\) injective), and every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\), there is a canonical
56	  isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
57	  \[
58	    (R^n G)(X)
59	      \;\cong\;
60	      H^n\bigl(G(I^\bullet)\bigr),
61	  \]
62	  where \(G(I^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex obtained by applying \(G\)
63	  degreewise to the resolution complex \(I^\bullet\) and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th
64	  cohomology object. This is the defining computation of the right-derived functor
65	  and the seed from which the acyclic-resolution generalization is bootstrapped.
66	\end{lemma}
67	
68	\begin{lemma}[Higher derived functors vanish on injectives]
69	  \label{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
70	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_rightDerived_obj_injective_succ}
71	  \mathlibok
72	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
73	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(J\) is an injective object of
74	  \(\mathcal{A}\) then \((R^{n+1} G)(J) = 0\) for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\); that
75	  is, the higher right-derived functors vanish on injective objects. In particular
76	  every injective object is right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of
77	  Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}.
78	\end{lemma}
79	
80	\begin{lemma}[The zeroth derived functor of a left-exact functor]
81	  \label{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
82	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedZeroIsoSelf}
83	  \mathlibok
84	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
85	  With \(\mathcal{A}, \mathcal{B}, G\) as above, if \(G\) is left exact (preserves
86	  finite limits) then the natural transformation \(G \to R^0 G\) is an isomorphism,
87	  \[
88	    R^0 G \;\cong\; G.
89	  \]
90	\end{lemma}
91	
92	\begin{lemma}[Long exact homology sequence of a short exact sequence of complexes]
93	  \label{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
94	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₁,
95	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₂,
96	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.homology_exact₃,
97	    CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.ShortExact.δ}
98	  \mathlibok
99	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
100	  Let \(\mathcal{C}\) be an abelian category and let
101	  \[
102	    0 \longrightarrow S.X_1 \longrightarrow S.X_2 \longrightarrow S.X_3
103	      \longrightarrow 0
104	  \]
105	  be a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{C}\) --- that is, a
106	  short complex \(S = (S.X_1 \to S.X_2 \to S.X_3)\) of objects of the category of
107	  \(\mathbb{Z}\)-indexed cochain complexes \(\mathrm{HomologicalComplex}\,\mathcal{C}\)
108	  which is short exact (degreewise mono, epi and exact in the middle). Then for every
109	  degree \(n\) there is a connecting morphism
110	  \[
111	    \delta^n \;:\; H^n(S.X_3) \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
112	  \]
113	  (the Mathlib map \texttt{ShortComplex.ShortExact.\(\delta\)}), and these assemble
114	  into a long exact sequence of homology objects in \(\mathcal{C}\)
115	  \[
116	    \cdots \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_2) \longrightarrow H^n(S.X_3)
117	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^n\ } H^{n+1}(S.X_1)
118	      \longrightarrow H^{n+1}(S.X_2) \longrightarrow \cdots .
119	  \]
120	  This is the complex-level homology long exact sequence and its connecting
121	  homomorphism. The derived-functor long exact sequence used in the sequel is
122	  constructed from this complex-level result via a horseshoe lift; see
123	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}.
124	\end{lemma}
125	
126	\section{Right-acyclic objects}
127	
128	\begin{definition}\leanok
129	[Right-\(G\)-acyclic object]
130	  \label{def:right_acyclic}
131	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.IsRightAcyclic}
132	  \uses{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
133	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Definition
134	  % \texttt{definition-derived-functor} (Tag 0157, items 3--4) and Lemma
135	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic} (Tag 015C, part 2)
136	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5264--5269 and
137	  % L5594--5617).
138	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "\item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
139	  % {\it right acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $RF$}
140	  % if $A[0]$ computes $RF$.
141	  % \item An object $A$ of $\mathcal{A}$ is said to be
142	  % {\it left acyclic for $F$}, or {\it acyclic for $LF$}
143	  % if $A[0]$ computes $LF$."
144	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
145	  % between abelian categories and assume
146	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
147	  % defined. Let $A$ be an object of $\mathcal{A}$.
148	  % \begin{enumerate}
149	  % \item $A$ is right acyclic for $F$ if and only if
150	  % $F(A) \to R^0F(A)$ is an isomorphism and $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$,
151	  % \item if $F$ is left exact, then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$
152	  % if and only if $R^iF(A) = 0$ for all $i > 0$.
153	  % \end{enumerate}"
154	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 0157
155	  (\texttt{definition-derived-functor}) and Tag 015C
156	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic}).}
157	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive functor between abelian
158	  categories. An object \(J\) of \(\mathcal{A}\) is \emph{right-\(G\)-acyclic} when
159	  the higher right-derived functors of \(G\) vanish at \(J\):
160	  \[
161	    (R^k G)(J) = 0 \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
162	  \]
163	  Equivalently, writing the condition with the index shifted, \((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\)
164	  for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\). The Stacks Project defines a right-\(G\)-acyclic
165	  object intrinsically (as one for which the one-term complex \(J[0]\) computes the
166	  derived functor \(RG\)); for an additive --- in our applications, left exact ---
167	  functor \(G\) that intrinsic condition is equivalent to the vanishing of all
168	  \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\), which is the form we adopt here and which is
169	  directly checkable against the injective-resolution computation of
170	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}.
171	
172	  \emph{Remark (alternative quantifier shapes).} Two equivalent formulations are
173	  available: ``\((R^k G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \geq 1\)'' or the index-shifted
174	  ``\((R^{k+1} G)(J) = 0\) for all \(k \in \mathbb{N}\)''; the latter avoids the
175	  inequality side-condition and aligns with the vanishing statement of
176	  Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}.
177	\end{definition}
178	
179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}
232	
233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}
269	
270	\begin{lemma}[Off-diagonal twist and augmentation lifts]
271	  \label{lem:horseshoe_twist}
272	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_cocycle, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.twistPair,
273	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ₁, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.f_comp_horseshoeβ₁,
274	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.g_comp_horseshoeH,
275	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeH_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero,
276	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτZero_hf, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeτ_zero,
277	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_fst,
278	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeβ_snd, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0,
279	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_d, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC0_comp_τZero,
280	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ιC_comp_horseshoeτZero}
281	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
282	  There exist an augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) and a family of morphisms
283	  \[
284	    \tau^n : I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N})
285	  \]
286	  --- the off-diagonal components of the middle differential --- satisfying the cocycle
287	  identity
288	  \[
289	    \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n \;=\; -\, d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \qquad (n \in \mathbb{N}),
290	  \]
291	  together with the compatibility of \(\beta\) with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\),
292	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and the maps of the original short exact sequence. Each \(\tau^n\) (and
293	  \(\beta\)) is produced by the universal lifting property of injectives: the target
294	  summand \(I_A^{n+1}\) is injective and the relevant source map is a monomorphism
295	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}).
296	\end{lemma}
297	
298	\begin{proof}
299	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
300	  The lifts are constructed by recursion on \(n\). For the augmentation, the first
301	  component of \(\beta\) extends the composite \(A \to I_A^0\) along the monomorphism
302	  \(A \hookrightarrow B\) using injectivity of \(I_A^0\); the second component is
303	  \(B \twoheadrightarrow C \to I_C^0\).
304	  At the inductive step we have \(\tau^{n}\) (with \(\tau^{n} : I_C^{n} \to I_A^{n+1}\))
305	  and seek \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) with
306	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\). The right-hand side
307	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n : I_C^n \to I_A^{n+2}\) annihilates
308	  \(\ker d_C^n = \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) --- the equality \(\ker d_C^n =
309	  \operatorname{im} d_C^{n-1}\) being the exactness of the injective resolution \(I_C\) in
310	  the positive degree \(n\), which is part of the defining data of an injective resolution.
311	  Indeed, precomposing with \(d_C^{n-1}\) and using the
312	  previous-stage identity \(\tau^{n} \circ d_C^{n-1} = -\,d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1}\) gives
313	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n \circ d_C^{n-1} = d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} \circ \tau^{n-1} = 0\),
314	  where the final equality is the complex identity \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^{n} = 0\) for the
315	  cochain complex \(I_A\). Hence
316	  \(-\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) factors through the image of \(d_C^n\), i.e.\ through the
317	  monomorphism \(\operatorname{im} d_C^n \hookrightarrow I_C^{n+1}\); injectivity of the
318	  target \(I_A^{n+2}\) extends that factorization along this monomorphism to a map
319	  \(\tau^{n+1} : I_C^{n+1} \to I_A^{n+2}\) defined on all of \(I_C^{n+1}\) and satisfying
320	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) by construction. This is exactly
321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.
322	\end{proof}
323	
324	\begin{lemma}\leanok
325	[The assembled middle differential squares to zero]
326	  \label{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
327	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_comp}
328	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
329	  With the twist family \(\tau\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, define the middle
330	  differential degreewise by the matrix
331	  \[
332	    d_B^n \;=\;
333	    \begin{pmatrix} d_A^n & \tau^n \\ 0 & d_C^n \end{pmatrix}
334	    \;:\; I_A^n \oplus I_C^n \longrightarrow I_A^{n+1} \oplus I_C^{n+1}.
335	  \]
336	  Then \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\) for every \(n\), so \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) is a
337	  cochain complex.
338	\end{lemma}
339	
340	\begin{proof}
341	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_twist}
342	  \leanok
343	  Multiplying the two matrices, the composite \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n\) has diagonal
344	  entries \(d_A^{n+1} \circ d_A^n = 0\) and \(d_C^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = 0\) (the complexes
345	  \(I_A\), \(I_C\) being complexes), lower-left entry \(0\), and upper-right entry
346	  \[
347	    d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n + \tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n,
348	  \]
349	  which vanishes precisely by the cocycle identity
350	  \(\tau^{n+1} \circ d_C^n = -\,d_A^{n+1} \circ \tau^n\) of
351	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. Hence \(d_B^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = 0\).
352	\end{proof}
353	
354	\begin{lemma}[The inclusion and projection are chain maps]
355	  \label{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}
356	  \lean{CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSplitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_shortExact,
357	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeMid, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES,
358	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeSES_splitting, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι,
359	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeι_f_zero, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.mono_horseshoeβ,
360	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_X, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprod_d,
361	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_fst, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodD_snd,
362	    CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodSnd_f, CategoryTheory.twistedBiprodInl_comp_Snd}
363	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp}
364	  With \(d_B\) as in Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, the degreewise biproduct
365	  coprojection \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n} : I_A^n \to I_B^n\) and projection
366	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n} : I_B^n \to I_C^n\) assemble into chain maps
367	  \(\iota : I_A^\bullet \to I_B^\bullet\) and \(\pi : I_B^\bullet \to I_C^\bullet\); that
368	  is, \(d_B^n \circ \iota^n = \iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\) and
369	  \(\pi^{n+1} \circ d_B^n = d_C^n \circ \pi^n\). Moreover \(\iota, \pi\) are compatible
370	  with the augmentations and form, in each degree, the split short exact sequence
371	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\) of
372	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
373	\end{lemma}
374	
375	\begin{proof}
376	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_dComp, lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
377	  These are direct biproduct computations from the matrix form of \(d_B\). Pre-composing
378	  \(d_B^n\) with the coprojection \(\iota^n\) of the first summand selects the first
379	  column \(\begin{pmatrix} d_A^n \\ 0\end{pmatrix}\), which equals
380	  \(\iota^{n+1} \circ d_A^n\); so \(\iota\) is a chain map. Post-composing \(d_B^n\) with
381	  the projection \(\pi^{n+1}\) onto the second summand selects the second row
382	  \(\begin{pmatrix} 0 & d_C^n\end{pmatrix}\), which equals \(d_C^n \circ \pi^n\); so
383	  \(\pi\) is a chain map. Compatibility with the augmentations is the corresponding
384	  statement in degree \(-1 \to 0\), immediate from the construction of \(\beta\) in
385	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}. In each degree \(\iota^n = \iota_{I_A^n}\) and
386	  \(\pi^n = \pi_{I_C^n}\) are the structure maps of the biproduct, so the degree is split
387	  exact by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}.
388	\end{proof}

390	\begin{lemma}\leanok
391	[Middle-term quasi-isomorphism transfer]
392	  \label{lem:quasiIso_tau2}
393	  \lean{HomologicalComplex.HomologySequence.quasiIso_τ₂}
394	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
395	  Project-local supplement (the \(\tau_2\) companion of Mathlib's \(\tau_3\) transfer
396	  \texttt{quasiIso\_τ₃}). Let \(\varphi : S_1 \to S_2\) be a morphism of short exact
397	  sequences of cochain complexes, with components \(\varphi.\tau_1, \varphi.\tau_2,
398	  \varphi.\tau_3\) on the three columns. Suppose \(\varphi.\tau_1\) and \(\varphi.\tau_3\)
399	  are quasi-isomorphisms, and that the middle component \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a
400	  monomorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no incoming differential, and an
401	  epimorphism in every degree \(i\) that has no outgoing differential. Then
402	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
403	\end{lemma}
404	
405	\begin{proof}
406	  \uses{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
407	  \leanok
408	  This is the homology four-lemma. Fix a degree \(i\); we show
409	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism by proving it is both epimorphic and
410	  monomorphic and concluding that a map which is both is an isomorphism. \emph{Epi:}
411	  when \(i\) has an outgoing differential to some \(j\), the two short exact sequences
412	  yield a commuting ladder whose rows are the five-term exact windows of the homology
413	  long exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) about degree
414	  \(i\); the four-lemma (epi form), using that \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1)\),
415	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are epi and \(H^j(\varphi.\tau_1)\) is mono, forces
416	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) epi. When \(i\) has no outgoing differential,
417	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is epi in degree \(i\) by hypothesis, and an epimorphism of
418	  complexes that is epi in a degree with no outgoing differential is epi on homology
419	  there. \emph{Mono:} dually, when \(i\) has an incoming differential from some \(k\),
420	  the four-lemma (mono form), using that \(H^k(\varphi.\tau_3)\) is epi and
421	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_1), H^i(\varphi.\tau_3)\) are mono, forces
422	  \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) mono; when \(i\) has no incoming differential it is mono in
423	  that degree by hypothesis, hence mono on homology. Being epi and mono in every
424	  degree, \(H^i(\varphi.\tau_2)\) is an isomorphism for all \(i\), so
425	  \(\varphi.\tau_2\) is a quasi-isomorphism.
426	\end{proof}
