428	\begin{lemma}[The middle complex resolves \(B\)]
429	  \label{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}
430	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact_resolvesMiddle,
431	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₁₂,
432	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_comm₂₃, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₁,
433	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₂, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.horseshoeφ_τ₃,
434	    CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.quasiIso_horseshoeι, CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.single₀_hom_ext}
435	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
436	  The cochain complex \((I_B^\bullet, d_B)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}, with the
437	  augmentation \(\beta : B \to I_B^0\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, is an injective
438	  resolution of \(B\): its terms are injective
439	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}), it is exact in every positive degree, and
440	  \(H^0 \cong B\) via \(\beta\).
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
445	    lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:quasiIso_tau2}
446	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap} the maps \(\iota, \pi\) form, degreewise, the
447	  split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_B^n \to I_C^n \to 0\); assembling these
448	  splittings (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}) gives a short exact sequence of
449	  cochain complexes
450	  \[
451	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^\bullet \longrightarrow I_B^\bullet
452	      \longrightarrow I_C^\bullet \longrightarrow 0 .
453	  \]
454	  Apply the complex-level homology long exact sequence
455	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}). The outer complexes \(I_A^\bullet\),
456	  \(I_C^\bullet\) are injective resolutions, hence exact in every positive degree
457	  (\(H^{k}(I_A) = H^{k}(I_C) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\)) with \(H^0(I_A) \cong A\),
458	  \(H^0(I_C) \cong C\). In the long exact sequence the terms flanking \(H^{k}(I_B)\) for
459	  \(k \geq 1\) vanish, forcing \(H^{k}(I_B) = 0\); in degree \(0\) the initial segment
460	  \(0 \to H^0(I_A) \to H^0(I_B) \to H^0(I_C) \to H^1(I_A) = 0\) reads
461	  \(0 \to A \to H^0(I_B) \to C \to 0\), which together with the original sequence
462	  \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\) and the augmentation \(\beta\) identifies
463	  \(H^0(I_B) \cong B\). Hence \(I_B^\bullet\) is exact in positive degrees and resolves
464	  \(B\); with injective terms it is an injective resolution of \(B\).
465	\end{proof}

467	\begin{lemma}\leanok
468	[Horseshoe lift of a short exact sequence to injective resolutions]
469	  \label{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
470	  \lean{CategoryTheory.InjectiveResolution.ofShortExact}
471	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
472	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
473	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
474	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}
475	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives and let
476	  \[
477	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow B \longrightarrow C \longrightarrow 0
478	  \]
479	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\). Then there exist injective
480	  resolutions \(I_A\) of \(A\), \(I_B\) of \(B\), \(I_C\) of \(C\), together with a
481	  short exact sequence of cochain complexes
482	  \[
483	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_B \longrightarrow I_C \longrightarrow 0
484	  \]
485	  which is compatible with the augmentations \(A \to I_A^0\), \(B \to I_B^0\),
486	  \(C \to I_C^0\) and which is \emph{degreewise split}: for every \(n\) the sequence
487	  \[
488	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n
489	      \longrightarrow 0
490	  \]
491	  is a split short exact sequence of \(\mathcal{A}\), so that
492	  \(I_B^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\).
493	\end{lemma}
494	
495	\begin{proof}
496	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective, lem:horseshoe_degree_split,
497	    lem:horseshoe_stage_mono, lem:horseshoe_twist, lem:horseshoe_dComp,
498	    lem:horseshoe_chainMap, lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle,
499	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
500	  \leanok
501	  This is the (dual) Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to
502	  Homological Algebra}, Lemma 2.2.8), assembled from the sub-lemmas of \S``The horseshoe
503	  construction, step by step''. Take \(I_A, I_C\) and set \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\):
504	  injective by Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}, degreewise split by
505	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}. The twist \(\tau\) and augmentation exist with
506	  the cocycle identity (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_twist}, via
507	  Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}); the matrix differential then squares to zero
508	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_dComp}) with coprojection and projection chain maps
509	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_chainMap}); and \(I_B^\bullet\) resolves \(B\)
510	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:horseshoe_resolvesMiddle}). These data are exactly the asserted
511	  degreewise-split short exact sequence \(0 \to I_A \to I_B \to I_C \to 0\) of cochain
512	  complexes compatible with the augmentations.
513	\end{proof}

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}[Resolution-level dimension shift]
529	  \label{lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
530	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfSplitResolutionSES, CategoryTheory.shortExact_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.shortExact_map_mapHomologicalComplex_of_degreewise_splitting, CategoryTheory.Functor.isZero_homology_mapHomologicalComplex_of_isRightAcyclic}
531	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
532	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
533	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
534	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
535	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
536	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
537	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
538	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
539	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
540	  \[
541	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
542	  \]
543	\end{lemma}
544	
545	\begin{proof}
546	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
547	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
548	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
549	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
550	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
551	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
552	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
553	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
554	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
555	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
556	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
557	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
558	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
559	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
560	\end{proof}
561	
562	\begin{lemma}\leanok
563	[Dimension shift across an acyclic short exact sequence]
564	  \label{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}
565	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedShiftIsoOfAcyclic}
566	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
567	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
568	    lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
569	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
570	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
571	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
572	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be a left exact functor
573	  % between abelian categories and assume
574	  % $RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \to D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$ is everywhere
575	  % defined. Let $0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0$ be a short exact sequence
576	  % of $\mathcal{A}$.
577	  % \begin{enumerate}
578	  % \item If $A$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $B$.
579	  % \item If $A$ and $B$ are right acyclic for $F$ then so is $C$.
580	  % \item If $B$ and $C$ are right acyclic for $F$ and $F(B) \to F(C)$ is
581	  % surjective then $A$ is right acyclic for $F$.
582	  % \end{enumerate}
583	  % In each of the three cases
584	  % $$
585	  % 0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to 0
586	  % $$
587	  % is a short exact sequence of $\mathcal{B}$."
588	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
589	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
590	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let
591	  \[
592	    0 \longrightarrow A \longrightarrow J \longrightarrow Z \longrightarrow 0
593	  \]
594	  be a short exact sequence in \(\mathcal{A}\) with the middle term \(J\)
595	  right-\(G\)-acyclic. Then the connecting maps of the long exact sequence of
596	  right-derived functors furnish isomorphisms
597	  \[
598	    (R^{k} G)(Z) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; (R^{k+1} G)(A)
599	    \qquad \text{for all } k \geq 1.
600	  \]
601	  (The companion lowest-degree statement
602	  \((R^{1} G)(A) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J) \to G(Z))\) is isolated as the
603	  sibling Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
604	\end{lemma}
605	
606	% The Stacks proof of Tag 015D derives the long exact sequence from the
607	% delta-functor structure of $RF$ on the derived category $D^{+}(\mathcal{A})$.
608	% The proof below constructs the same sequence at the level of right-derived-functor
609	% objects from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
610	% (Lemma \ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) after a horseshoe lift
611	% (Lemma \ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}); accordingly no verbatim proof
612	% quote from Tag 015D is attached.
613	\begin{proof}
614	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
615	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
616	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses, lem:right_derived_shift_split_resolution}
617	    \leanok
618	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
619	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
620	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
621	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
622	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.
623	
624	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
625	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
626	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
627	  complexes
628	  \[
629	    0 \longrightarrow I_A \longrightarrow I_J \longrightarrow I_Z \longrightarrow 0
630	  \]
631	  that is degreewise split. Apply \(G\) degreewise. Because each degree
632	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence
633	  (so \(I_J^n \cong I_A^n \oplus I_Z^n\) compatibly with the maps), the additive
634	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
635	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
636	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
637	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence
638	  \[
639	    0 \longrightarrow G(I_A) \longrightarrow G(I_J) \longrightarrow G(I_Z)
640	      \longrightarrow 0
641	  \]
642	  is a short exact sequence of cochain complexes in \(\mathcal{B}\). The
643	  complex-level homology long exact sequence
644	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) applied to it reads
645	  \[
646	    \cdots \to H^k\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to H^k\bigl(G(I_Z)\bigr)
647	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_A)\bigr)
648	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
649	  \]
650	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution} the homology of each applied
651	  resolution computes the right-derived functor:
652	  \(H^k(G(I_A)) \cong (R^k G)(A)\), \(H^k(G(I_J)) \cong (R^k G)(J)\) and
653	  \(H^k(G(I_Z)) \cong (R^k G)(Z)\), naturally in the maps of the sequence. Transporting
654	  the homology long exact sequence across these isomorphisms gives the long exact
655	  sequence of right-derived functors
656	  \[
657	    \cdots \to (R^k G)(J) \to (R^k G)(Z)
658	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to (R^{k+1} G)(J) \to \cdots,
659	  \]
660	  and, using \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) at the
661	  bottom, its low-degree initial segment
662	  \[
663	    0 \to G(A) \to G(J) \to G(Z)
664	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J) \to \cdots .
665	  \]
666	
667	  \emph{Killing the acyclic terms.} Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic
668	  (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), every term \((R^k G)(J)\) with \(k \geq 1\)
669	  vanishes. For \(k \geq 1\) the segment around \((R^k G)(J)\),
670	  \[
671	    \underbrace{(R^{k} G)(J)}_{=0} \to (R^{k} G)(Z)
672	      \xrightarrow{\ \delta^k\ } (R^{k+1} G)(A) \to \underbrace{(R^{k+1} G)(J)}_{=0},
673	  \]
674	  has both outer terms zero, so the connecting map
675	  \(\delta^k : (R^{k} G)(Z) \to (R^{k+1} G)(A)\) is an isomorphism, which is the
676	  asserted dimension shift. (The lowest-degree segment of the same long exact
677	  sequence yields the cokernel description of \((R^1 G)(A)\); that reading is
678	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
679	\end{proof}
680	
681	\begin{lemma}\leanok

684	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedOneIsoCokerOfAcyclic}

703	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
704	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be an additive left-exact functor, and let

717	\begin{proof}
718	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self,
719	    lem:right_derived_injective_resolution, lem:homology_long_exact_sequence,
720	    lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}
721	    \leanok
722	  This reads off the lowest-degree segment of the very long exact sequence of
723	  right-derived functors built, from the same short exact sequence
724	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\), in the proof of
725	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift}; here we use only its initial terms, so we
726	  recall the construction briefly and then specialize. By the horseshoe lift
727	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
728	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a degreewise-split short exact
729	  sequence of cochain complexes \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\). Applying the
730	  additive functor \(G\) degreewise preserves each split degree, giving a short exact
731	  sequence of complexes \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in
732	  \(\mathcal{B}\); its complex-level homology long exact sequence
733	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}), transported across the
734	  injective-resolution computation \(H^k(G(I_\bullet)) \cong (R^k G)(-)\)
735	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}) and using
736	  \(R^0 G \cong G\) (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}), has low-degree
737	  initial segment
738	  \[
739	    G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to (R^1 G)(J).
740	  \]
741	  Because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}), the right
742	  end \((R^1 G)(J)\) vanishes. Hence the segment
743	  \(G(J) \to G(Z) \xrightarrow{\ \delta^0\ } (R^1 G)(A) \to 0\) is exact with vanishing
744	  right end, so \(\delta^0\) is an epimorphism whose kernel is the image of
745	  \(G(J) \to G(Z)\). Therefore \(\delta^0\) factors through an isomorphism
746	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J) \to G(Z)\bigr) \xrightarrow{\ \cong\ } (R^1 G)(A)\),
747	  which is the assertion.
748	\end{proof}
749	
750	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
751	
752	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
753	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
754	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
755	here.
756	
757	\begin{lemma}[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
758	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}
759	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortExact, CategoryTheory.Functor.cosyzygyShortComplex, CategoryTheory.cosyzygyKernelFork, CategoryTheory.cosyzygy_iCycles_comp_toCycles, CategoryTheory.epi_toCycles_of_exactAt}
760	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
761	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
762	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
763	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838).
764	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
765	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
766	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
767	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
768	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
769	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
770	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
771	  \[
772	    0 \longrightarrow A
773	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
774	      \longrightarrow \cdots
775	  \]
776	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
777	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
778	  \[
779	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
780	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
781	  \]
782	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
783	  \[
784	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
785	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
786	  \]
787	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
788	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
789	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.
790	  In particular \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\).
791	\end{lemma}
792	
793	\begin{proof}
794	  \uses{def:right_acyclic}
795	  Fix \(m \geq 0\). Mono of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) is immediate: \(Z^m\) is by
796	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
797	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
798	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
799	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
800	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
801	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
802	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
803	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
804	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
805	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
806	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
807	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final
808	  identity \(Z^1 = \operatorname{im}(J^0 \to J^1) = \operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0)\)
809	  is the case \(m = 0\): \(\operatorname{coker}(A \to J^0) = \operatorname{coker}(Z^0 \to J^0)
810	  \cong Z^1\) by \((S_0)\).
811	\end{proof}
812	
813	\begin{lemma}[Applied cosyzygy is the cocycle object of the applied complex]
814	  \label{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}
815	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_hom_iCycles, CategoryTheory.Functor.gCosyzygyIsoCocycles_toCycles}
816	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
817	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
818	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D) — the left-exact initial segment
819	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C)$ specialized to the cosyzygy sequences
820	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5619--5654).
821	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "we obtain an exact cohomology sequence
822	  % $0 \to F(A) \to F(B) \to F(C) \to R^1F(A) \to \ldots$
823	  % in the abelian category $\mathcal{B}$."
824	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015D
825	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}), left-exact initial segment.}
826	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
827	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, for every \(n \geq 0\) the morphism \(G\) applies to
828	  the inclusion \(Z^n \hookrightarrow J^n\) to give an isomorphism
829	  \[
830	    G(Z^n) \;\xrightarrow{\ \cong\ }\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr),
831	  \]
832	  identifying \(G(Z^n)\), as a subobject of \(G(J^n)\), with the \(n\)-th cocycle
833	  object of the applied complex \(G(J^\bullet)\). For \(n = 0\) this reads
834	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)\).
835	\end{lemma}
836	
837	\begin{proof}
838	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
839	  Fix \(n \geq 0\). Apply the left-exact functor \(G\) to the cosyzygy short exact
840	  sequence \((S_n)\), \(0 \to Z^n \to J^n \to Z^{n+1} \to 0\) of
841	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}; left-exactness yields an exact sequence
842	  \[
843	    0 \longrightarrow G(Z^n) \longrightarrow G(J^n) \longrightarrow G(Z^{n+1}),
844	  \]
845	  so \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) is the kernel of \(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})\).
846	  Next, the differential \(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\) of the applied complex factors as
847	  \(G(J^n) \xrightarrow{G(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1})} G(Z^{n+1})
848	  \xrightarrow{G(Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1})} G(J^{n+1})\), because the original
849	  differential \(J^n \to J^{n+1}\) factors through its image \(Z^{n+1}\) as
850	  \(J^n \twoheadrightarrow Z^{n+1} \hookrightarrow J^{n+1}\) and \(G\) preserves
851	  composition. The second factor \(G(Z^{n+1}) \to G(J^{n+1})\) is a monomorphism (the
852	  image of the left-exact application of \(G\) to \((S_{n+1})\), whose first map
853	  \(G(Z^{n+1}) \hookrightarrow G(J^{n+1})\) is mono). A monomorphism does not change
854	  kernels under precomposition, so
855	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) = \ker(G(J^n) \to G(Z^{n+1})) = G(Z^n)\). Hence
856	  \(G(Z^n) \xrightarrow{\cong} \ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1}))\) as subobjects of
857	  \(G(J^n)\). For \(n = 0\), \(Z^0 = A\) gives
858	  \(G(A) \cong \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1)) = H^0(G(J^\bullet))\), the last equality being
859	  the definition of the zeroth cohomology of a cochain complex starting in degree
860	  \(0\) (no incoming differential). The use of \(R^0 G \cong G\)
861	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) is what licenses reading \(G(A)\) as
862	  \((R^0 G)(A)\) when this identification feeds the comparison theorem.
863	\end{proof}
864	
865	\begin{lemma}[Cohomology of the applied resolution as a cosyzygy cokernel]
866	  \label{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution}
867	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso, CategoryTheory.Functor.gHomologyZeroIso}
868	  % NOTE (iter-007 review): this lemma states BOTH the degree-0 case (H⁰ ≅ G(A)) and the
869	  % positive-degree case (Hⁿ ≅ coker). The Lean split is unavoidable — the n=0 and n≥1 targets
870	  % have different types — so the \lean{} names both decls: cohomologyAppliedResolutionIso (n≥1)
871	  % and gHomologyZeroIso (n=0). Both are sorry-free + axiom-clean.
872	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
873	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Proposition
874	  % \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA) and Lemma
875	  % \texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses} (Tag 015D)
876	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5785--5838 and L5619--5654).
877	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
878	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
879	  % $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
880	  % for $n \geq n_0$."
881	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 05TA
882	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}) and Tag 015D
883	  (\texttt{lemma-F-acyclic-ses}).}
884	  With \(G\) additive left exact and the acyclic resolution and cosyzygies of
885	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}, the cohomology of the applied complex
886	  \(G(J^\bullet) = \bigl(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to \cdots\bigr)\) is computed by
887	  \[
888	    H^0\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\; G(A),
889	    \qquad
890	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr) \;\cong\;
891	      \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr) \quad (n \geq 1),
892	  \]
893	  where \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) is \(G\) applied to the surjection
894	  \(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n\) of \((S_{n-1})\).
895	\end{lemma}
896	
897	\begin{proof}
898	  \uses{lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles, lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}
899	  The degree-zero statement is the \(n = 0\) case of
900	  Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles}: \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) = \ker(G(J^0) \to G(J^1))
901	  \cong G(Z^0) = G(A)\). Fix now \(n \geq 1\). By definition of the cohomology of the
902	  cochain complex \(G(J^\bullet)\) in positive degree,
903	  \[
904	    H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr)
905	      \;=\; \ker\bigl(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})\bigr) \big/
906	            \operatorname{im}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\bigr).
907	  \]
908	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:applied_cosyzygy_cycles} the numerator is
909	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) \cong G(Z^n)\), realized as the subobject
910	  \(G(Z^n) \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\). Under this identification the differential
911	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n)\) lands inside \(G(Z^n)\): it factors as
912	  \(G(J^{n-1}) \xrightarrow{G(J^{n-1} \twoheadrightarrow Z^n)} G(Z^n)
913	  \hookrightarrow G(J^n)\) by the factorization of \(J^{n-1} \to J^n\) through its image
914	  \(Z^n\), so its image equals the image of \(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\) inside the
915	  subobject \(G(Z^n)\). Therefore the quotient
916	  \(\ker(G(J^n) \to G(J^{n+1})) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(J^n))\) is exactly
917	  \(G(Z^n) / \operatorname{im}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) =
918	  \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\). Hence
919	  \(H^n(G(J^\bullet)) \cong \operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n))\).
920	\end{proof}
921	
922	\section{The acyclic-resolution comparison theorem}
923	
924	\begin{theorem}\leanok
925	[An acyclic resolution computes the right-derived functor]
926	  \label{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived}
927	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Functor.rightDerivedIsoOfAcyclicResolution}
928	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
929	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
930	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
931	  % NOTE (iter-009 review): the Lean encodes the augmentation in the
932	  % "augmentation-dropped" form `(K, e : A ≅ K.cycles 0, hexact : ∀ n, K.ExactAt (n+1))`,
933	  % equivalent to the blueprint's augmented complex `A → K•`; left-exactness of `G`
934	  % is `[PreservesFiniteLimits G]`. Verified faithful by the iter-009
935	  % lean-vs-blueprint-checker (PASS, 0 red flags).
936	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Derived Categories, Lemma
937	  % \texttt{lemma-leray-acyclicity} (Tag 015E, Leray's acyclicity lemma) and
938	  % Proposition \texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics} (Tag 05TA)
939	  % (read from references/homological-acyclic-derived.tex, L5692--5705 and
940	  % L5785--5811).
941	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor
942	  % between abelian categories. Let $A^\bullet$ be a bounded below complex
943	  % of right $F$-acyclic objects such that $RF$ is defined at
944	  % $A^\bullet$. The canonical map
945	  % $$
946	  % F(A^\bullet) \longrightarrow RF(A^\bullet)
947	  % $$
948	  % is an isomorphism in $D^{+}(\mathcal{B})$, i.e., $A^\bullet$ computes
949	  % $RF$."
950	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $F : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$ be an additive functor of
951	  % abelian categories. ... If every object of $\mathcal{A}$ injects into an object
952	  % acyclic for $RF$, then $RF$ is defined on all of $K^{+}(\mathcal{A})$
953	  % and we obtain an exact functor
954	  % $$
955	  % RF : D^{+}(\mathcal{A}) \longrightarrow D^{+}(\mathcal{B})
956	  % $$
957	  % ... Moreover, any bounded below complex
958	  % $A^\bullet$ whose terms are acyclic for $RF$ computes $RF$."
959	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Derived Categories, Tag 015E (Leray's acyclicity
960	  lemma) and Tag 05TA (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
961	  Let \(\mathcal{A}\) be an abelian category with enough injectives, \(\mathcal{B}\)
962	  abelian, and \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) an additive left-exact functor.
963	  Let
964	  \[
965	    0 \longrightarrow A
966	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
967	      \longrightarrow \cdots
968	  \]
969	  be a resolution of \(A\) (an exact sequence) in which every object \(J^n\) is
970	  right-\(G\)-acyclic in the sense of Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}. Then for
971	  every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\) the \(n\)-th right-derived functor of \(G\) at \(A\) is
972	  computed by the resolution: there is an isomorphism in \(\mathcal{B}\)
973	  \[
974	    (R^n G)(A) \;\cong\; H^n\bigl(G(J^\bullet)\bigr),
975	  \]
976	  where \(G(J^\bullet)\) is the cochain complex
977	  \(G(J^0) \to G(J^1) \to G(J^2) \to \cdots\) obtained by applying \(G\) degreewise
978	  to the augmentation-dropped resolution and \(H^n\) is its \(n\)-th cohomology
979	  object.
980	\end{theorem}
981	
982	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
983	% $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences
984	% $0 \to J^n \to I^{n + 1} \to J^{n + 1} \to 0$
985	% for $n \geq n_0$. These sequences induce distinguished triangles
986	% $(J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1})$ in $D^+(\mathcal{A})$ by
987	% Lemma \ref{lemma-derived-canonical-delta-functor}.
988	% ... By Proposition \ref{proposition-derived-functor} we have a
989	% distinguished triangle $(RF(J^n), RF(I^{n + 1}), RF(J^{n + 1}))$.
990	% Since $J^n, I^{n + 1}, J^{n + 1}$ are in $\mathcal{I}$
991	% the long exact cohomology sequence
992	% ... associated to this distinguished triangle collapses
993	% to an exact sequence
994	% $$
995	% 0 \to F(J^n) \to F(I^{n + 1}) \to F(J^{n + 1}) \to 0
996	% $$
997	% This in turn proves that $F(I^\bullet)$ is exact."
998	\begin{proof}
999	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:cosyzygy_ses, lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution,
1000	    lem:acyclic_dimension_shift, lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker,
1001	    lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self, lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}
1002	    \leanok
1003	  The argument is the classical dimension shift, now assembled from the cosyzygy
1004	  infrastructure and the dimension-shift step proved above. Cut the resolution into
1005	  its cosyzygy short exact sequences \((S_m) : 0 \to Z^m \to J^m \to Z^{m+1} \to 0\)
1006	  with \(Z^0 = A\) and acyclic middle terms, as furnished by
1007	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cosyzygy_ses}.
1008	
1009	  \emph{Degree zero.} By Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution},
1010	  \(H^0(G(J^\bullet)) \cong G(A)\), and by \(R^0 G \cong G\)
1011	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_zero_iso_self}) the right-hand side is \((R^0 G)(A)\).
1012	  This is the case \(n = 0\).
1013	
1014	  \emph{Positive degrees.} Fix \(n \geq 1\). The sequences
1015	  \((S_0), \ldots, (S_{n-2})\) all have acyclic middle terms, so the dimension-shift
1016	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} applied to them gives, for each \(k \geq 1\),
1017	  isomorphisms \((R^k G)(Z^{m+1}) \cong (R^{k+1} G)(Z^m)\) for \(0 \le m \le n-2\)
1018	  (with \(Z^0 = A\)). Composing them down the staircase
1019	  \[
1020	    (R^n G)(A)
1021	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-1} G)(Z^1)
1022	      \;\cong\; (R^{n-2} G)(Z^2)
1023	      \;\cong\; \cdots
1024	      \;\cong\; (R^{1} G)(Z^{n-1})
1025	  \]
1026	  reduces \((R^n G)(A)\) to a single first-derived functor. (For \(n = 1\) the
1027	  staircase is empty and \((R^1 G)(A) = (R^1 G)(Z^0)\).) Now apply the lowest-degree
1028	  cokernel description (Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}) to \((S_{n-1})\),
1029	  \(0 \to Z^{n-1} \to J^{n-1} \to Z^n \to 0\), whose middle term \(J^{n-1}\) is
1030	  acyclic:
1031	  \[
1032	    (R^1 G)(Z^{n-1}) \;\cong\; \operatorname{coker}\bigl(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)\bigr).
1033	  \]
1034	  Finally Lemma~\ref{lem:cohomology_of_applied_resolution} identifies this cokernel
1035	  with the cohomology of the applied complex,
1036	  \(\operatorname{coker}(G(J^{n-1}) \to G(Z^n)) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\). Composing
1037	  the staircase, the cokernel description, and this identification gives
1038	  \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(J^\bullet))\) for every \(n \geq 1\); together with the
1039	  degree-zero case this proves the theorem for every \(n \in \mathbb{N}\).
1040	
1041	  Every long exact sequence consumed above is the one produced by the dimension-shift
1042	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_dimension_shift} and its lowest-degree companion
1043	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}, and is therefore grounded --- through the
1044	  horseshoe lift and the complex-level homology long exact sequence on which those
1045	  lemmas rest --- on the injective-resolution computation of \(R^n G\). The injective
1046	  objects supplied by ``enough injectives'' are themselves right-\(G\)-acyclic
1047	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_vanishes_injective}), which is what makes an injective
1048	  resolution a special case of an acyclic resolution and reconciles the present
1049	  formula with the defining computation \((R^n G)(A) \cong H^n(G(I^\bullet))\) for an
1050	  injective resolution \(I^\bullet\).
1051	\end{proof}
1052

200	\paragraph{(2) Push--pull functor — lifting the backbone to modules.}
201	The geometric backbone is turned into a cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module by
202	the \emph{relative-direct-image functor on the over-category}
203	\[
204	  G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
205	  \qquad
206	  (Y \xrightarrow{\,p\,} X) \;\longmapsto\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
207	\]
208	sending an \(X\)-scheme \(p : Y \to X\) to the pushforward along \(p\) of the
209	pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\). Composing \(G\) with the (opposite of the) simplicial
210	backbone of (1) produces exactly the augmented cosimplicial object of
211	Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: on the degree-\(p\) piece
212	\(p : U_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \hookrightarrow X\) one has
213	\(p_* p^*\mathcal{F} = (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\),
214	and a morphism of \(X\)-schemes \(h : (Y_1, p_1) \to (Y_2, p_2)\) (so
215	\(h \circ p_2 = p_1\)\,, reading \(p\) as the structure map) induces the
216	restriction comparison \(G(p_2) \to G(p_1)\) via the unit/counit of the
217	pull-push adjunction together with the over-triangle identifying the two
218	structure maps. This functor \(G\) is the \emph{one non-formal step} in the whole
219	construction. Its functoriality --- that \(G\) respects identities and composition
220	of morphisms over \(X\) --- is precisely a statement about the coherence of the
221	comparison isomorphisms \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) and
222	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\) for pushforward and pullback of sheaves of
223	modules, together with their compatibility with the adjunction units. Concretely,
224	pullback and pushforward of sheaves of modules are organised as a
225	pseudofunctor \(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to
226	\mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\) (sending a morphism to the adjoint pair
227	\(p^* \dashv p_*\)), and the comparison isomorphisms
228	\((p \circ q)^* \cong q^* p^*\), \((p \circ q)_* \cong p_* q_*\) are its structure
229	\(2\)-cells. The functoriality of \(G\) needs exactly three facts: the mate identity
230	expressing the conjugate of the inverse pullback comparison as the pushforward
231	comparison, its normalisation at the identity morphism, and the pseudofunctor
232	unitor and pentagon (associativity) coherences. The laws are nonetheless not formal
233	trivialities: each is a multi-step mate calculation that transports the comparison
234	isomorphisms along the over-triangle \(g \circ p_2 = p_1\) of structure maps and
235	past the adjunction unit, transporting along the over-triangle equality; the
236	obstacle is the bookkeeping of those transports, not a missing geometric ingredient.
237	
238	\paragraph{(3) Coherence-free plumbing — from the nerve to the cochain complex.}
239	The passage from the augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module of (1)--(2)
240	to the relative {\v C}ech complex in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) is again purely
241	formal and uses \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence. One forgets the
242	augmentation, pushes the cosimplicial object forward along \(f : X \to S\) (a
243	covariant operation applied degreewise), and takes the alternating-coface-map
244	cochain complex of the resulting cosimplicial object of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules.
245	This step relies only on the preadditivity of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\): the
246	alternating sum \(\sum_{j} (-1)^j d^j\) of the cofaces is defined in any
247	preadditive category and squares to zero by the cosimplicial identities. Thus the
248	relative {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\)
249	of Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} is genuinely \emph{defined} in terms of the
250	{\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}: once the nerve is constructed,
251	the complex follows with no further input. In summary, of the three pieces only
252	the push--pull functor (2) requires content beyond formal category theory, and
253	that content is the pseudofunctor coherence of pushforward and pullback: the
254	comparison \(2\)-cells of the pseudofunctor
255	\(\mathrm{LocallyDiscrete}(\mathbf{Sch}^{\mathrm{op}}) \to \mathbf{Adj}\,\mathbf{Cat}\)
256	and their unitor/pentagon identities.
257	
258	\paragraph{Formal bricks of the three-part construction.}
259	The geometric backbone (1), the push--pull functor (2), and the plumbing (3) are
260	recorded as the following declarations. The backbone and plumbing are
261	unconditional; the push--pull object and morphism maps are likewise unconditional
262	bricks, and the functor laws assembling them are the lone coherence statement.
263	
264	\begin{definition}\leanok
265	[Arrow of an affine cover]
266	  \label{def:cover_arrow}
267	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverArrow}
268	  Let \(\mathcal{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be an affine open cover. Its
269	  \emph{cover arrow} is the single morphism
270	  \(\coprod_{i \in I} U_i \longrightarrow X\) obtained as the universal map out of
271	  the coproduct induced by the open immersions \(U_i \hookrightarrow X\). Packaging
272	  the cover as one arrow lets the generic {\v C}ech-nerve-of-an-arrow machinery
273	  apply.
274	\end{definition}
275	
276	\begin{definition}[Augmented {\v C}ech nerve of a cover]
277	  \label{def:cover_cech_nerve}
278	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerve, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOver, AlgebraicGeometry.coverCechNerveOverAug}
279	  \uses{def:cover_arrow}
280	  The augmented {\v C}ech nerve of the cover arrow of
281	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_arrow}: the augmented \emph{simplicial scheme} over
282	  \(X\) whose degree-\(p\) object is the \((p+1)\)-fold fibre power
283	  \[
284	    \Bigl(\coprod_{i} U_i\Bigr)^{\times_X (p+1)}
285	      = \coprod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
286	        U_{i_0} \times_X \cdots \times_X U_{i_p},
287	  \]
288	  with augmentation the cover map to \(X\). It exists unconditionally because the
289	  category of schemes has all finite limits (hence the wide pullbacks underlying the
290	  {\v C}ech nerve of an arrow). This is the geometric backbone (1).
291	\end{definition}
292	
293	\begin{definition}\leanok
294	[Push--pull object map]
295	  \label{def:push_pull_obj}
296	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullObj}
297	  The object map of the push--pull functor \(G\): to an \(X\)-scheme
298	  \(p : Y \to X\) it assigns
299	  \[
300	    G(Y, p) \;=\; p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F},
301	  \]
302	  the pushforward along \(p\) of the pullback \(p^*\mathcal{F}\).
303	\end{definition}
304	
305	\begin{definition}[Push--pull comparison map]
306	  \label{def:push_pull_map}
307	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_eq_raw, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_self_gen, AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardComp_hom_app_id, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_comp}
308	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj}
309	  The morphism map of \(G\). A morphism \(g : (Y_2, p_2) \to (Y_1, p_1)\) of
310	  \(X\)-schemes has underlying map \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) satisfying the
311	  over-triangle \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\); the contravariant functor produces the
312	  restriction comparison \(G(Y_1, p_1) \to G(Y_2, p_2)\) as the five-step composite
313	  \[
314	    p_{1*}\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
315	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{1*}(\eta)\,}
316	    p_{1*}\,\bar g_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
317	      \xrightarrow{\,\sim\,}
318	    (p_1 \circ \bar g)_*\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
319	      \;=\;
320	    p_{2*}\,\bar g^*\,p_1^*\mathcal{F}
321	      \xrightarrow{\,p_{2*}(\sim)\,}
322	    p_{2*}\,p_2^*\mathcal{F},
323	  \]
324	  where \(\eta\) is the unit of the adjunction \(\bar g^* \dashv \bar g_*\), the
325	  second arrow is the pushforward comparison \(p_{1*}\bar g_* \cong (p_1 \bar g)_*\),
326	  the equality is the substitution \(p_2 = p_1 \circ \bar g\) of the over-triangle,
327	  and the last arrow applies \(p_{2*}\) to the pullback comparison
328	  \(\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (p_1 \bar g)^* = p_2^*\). The two over-triangle
329	  substitutions are realised as coercions along \(p_1 \circ \bar g = p_2\).
330	\end{definition}
331	
332	\begin{lemma}
333	
334	\leanok
335	[Push--pull functor — identity law]
336	  \label{lem:push_pull_id}
337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_id}
338	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map}
339	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} respects identities:
340	  \(G(\mathrm{id}_Y) = \mathrm{id}_{G(Y)}\) for every \(X\)-scheme \(Y\).
341	\end{lemma}
342	
343	\begin{proof}
344	
345	\leanok
346	  A direct calculation with comparison isomorphisms, with no sectionwise content.
347	  Rewrite the head \(p_*(\eta)\) followed by the pushforward comparison as the
348	  conjugate-mate of the inverse pullback comparison; the identity leg then collapses
349	  by the identity normalisation of the pseudofunctor together with right unitality,
350	  and the two over-triangle transport coercions cancel against the unitality coercion.
351	\end{proof}
352	
353	\begin{lemma}
354	[Push--pull functor — composition law]
355	  \label{lem:push_pull_comp}
356	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.rawPushPullMap_comp, AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_pentagon}
357	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
358	  The morphism map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} is contravariantly functorial:
359	  for composable morphisms \(h : Y_3 \to Y_2\), \(g : Y_2 \to Y_1\) of \(X\)-schemes,
360	  \[
361	    G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g).
362	  \]
363	  Together with Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} this assembles
364	  Definitions~\ref{def:push_pull_obj}--\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a functor
365	  \(G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \to \operatorname{QCoh}(X)\); composing \(G\) with
366	  the backbone of Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} yields the {\v C}ech nerve of
367	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}.
368	\end{lemma}
369	
370	\begin{proof}
371	  \uses{lem:push_pull_unit_mate, lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
372	  The composition identity \(G(g \circ h) = G(h) \circ G(g)\) follows from three
373	  coherence facts. First, the composite adjunction unit \(\eta^{g \circ h}\) decomposes into
374	  the iterated units \(\eta^{g}\), \(\eta^{h}\) via the mate identity of
375	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_unit_mate} and the naturality of the adjunction unit. Second,
376	  the resulting pullback-side identity is the \emph{pentagon coherence} of the pullback
377	  pseudofunctor — the associativity \(2\)-cell relating the two bracketings of the comparison
378	  isomorphism \((p \circ q \circ r)^* \cong r^* q^* p^*\). Third, the over-triangle transport
379	  coherences are absorbed by Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}.
380	\end{proof}
381	
382	\begin{lemma}
383	
384	\leanok
385	[Base-change unit (mate) identity for the push--pull head]
386	  \label{lem:push_pull_unit_mate}
387	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_unit_mate}
388	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
389	  For composable scheme morphisms \(f : A \to B\), \(p : B \to Z\) and a
390	  \(\mathcal{O}_Z\)-module \(N\), the adjunction unit \(\eta^p_N\) of
391	  \(p^* \dashv p_*\) at \(N\), followed by \(p_*\) of the unit \(\eta^f\) for
392	  \(f^* \dashv f_*\) at \(p^*N\), followed by the pushforward comparison
393	  \(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\), equals the unit \(\eta^{f \circ p}_N\) for the
394	  composite followed by \((f \circ p)_*\) applied to the inverse pullback
395	  comparison \((f \circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\):
396	  \[
397	    \eta^p_N \;\circ\; p_*(\eta^f_{p^*N}) \;\circ\;
398	      \bigl(p_* f_* \cong (f \circ p)_*\bigr)
399	    \;=\;
400	    \eta^{f \circ p}_N \;\circ\; (f \circ p)_*\bigl((f\circ p)^* \cong f^* p^*\bigr)^{-1}.
401	  \]
402	  This is the mate-calculus core that converts the single-morphism unit
403	  \(\eta^{f \circ p}\) into the iterated units \(\eta^p, \eta^f\); it is the
404	  reusable ingredient consumed by the functoriality (pentagon) law of the
405	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map}.
406	\end{lemma}
407	
408	\begin{proof}
409	
410	\leanok
411	  A direct mate-calculus computation: unfold both sides using the definition of
412	  the adjunction unit for the composite and the pseudofunctor structure maps, then
413	  verify the equality by the mate identity for the pushforward/pullback comparison.
414	\end{proof}
415	
416	\begin{lemma}
417	
418	\leanok
419	[Over-triangle transport cancellation for the push--pull tail]
420	  \label{lem:push_pull_transport_cancel}
421	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPull_transport_cancel}
422	  \uses{def:push_pull_map}
423	  Let \(\bar g : Y_2 \to Y_1\), \(p_1 : Y_1 \to X\), \(p_2 : Y_2 \to X\) satisfy the
424	  over-triangle \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\), and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a
425	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module. Then the pullback-comparison leg of the push--pull map,
426	  conjugated by the two \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions along the over-triangle,
427	  collapses to the transport-light form
428	  \[
429	    e_1 \;\circ\; p_{2*}\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
430	      \;\circ\; e_2
431	    \;=\;
432	    (\bar g p_1)_*\bigl((\bar g^* p_1^* \cong (\bar g p_1)^*)_{\mathcal{F}}\bigr)
433	      \;\circ\; e_3,
434	  \]
435	  where \(e_1, e_2, e_3\) are the \(\mathrm{eqToHom}\) coercions induced by
436	  \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\). It states the over-triangle cancellation in the
437	  push--pull comparison map of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} with the
438	  over-triangle equality as a free hypothesis, so a single substitution turns the
439	  transports into identities; it is the reusable pre-coherence brick for the
440	  composition (pentagon) law.
441	\end{lemma}
442	
443	\begin{proof}
444	
445	\leanok
446	  Substitute the over-triangle equality \(\bar g \circ p_1 = p_2\) to replace
447	  transport coercions by identities, then verify the cancellation by direct
448	  calculation with the pushforward comparison.
449	\end{proof}
450	
451	\begin{definition}
452	
453	\leanok
454	[Push--pull functor]
455	  \label{def:push_pull_functor}
456	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushPullFunctor}
457	  \uses{def:push_pull_obj, def:push_pull_map, lem:push_pull_id, lem:push_pull_comp}
458	  The \emph{push--pull functor}
459	  \[
460	    G : (X/\mathbf{Sch})^{\mathrm{op}} \longrightarrow \operatorname{QCoh}(X),
461	  \]
462	  assembling the object map \(p \mapsto p_*\, p^*\mathcal{F}\) of
463	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_obj} and the comparison morphism map of
464	  Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_map} into a genuine functor. Functoriality is exactly the two
465	  laws established above: the identity law
466	  Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_id} and the composition law Lemma~\ref{lem:push_pull_comp}. The
467	  functor is contravariant in the over-category \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\); it is the lift of the
468	  geometric backbone (1) to \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules described as step (2) above.
469	\end{definition}
470	
471	\begin{definition}
472	
473	\leanok
474	[{\v C}ech nerve cosimplicial object]
475	  \label{def:cech_nerve_cosimplicial}
476	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechNerveCosimplicial}
477	  \uses{def:cover_cech_nerve, def:push_pull_functor}
478	  The (non-augmented) cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module obtained by transporting the
479	  geometric {\v C}ech backbone in \((X/\mathbf{Sch})\) of
480	  Definition~\ref{def:cover_cech_nerve} through the push--pull functor
481	  \(G\) of Definition~\ref{def:push_pull_functor}: degreewise, the \(p\)-th term is the
482	  product over \((i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}\) of the \((p+1)\)-fold-intersection
483	  pushforwards \((j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}})\). This is the
484	  underlying cosimplicial object that the {\v C}ech nerve of
485	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} augments by the structure map \(\mathcal{F} \to
486	  \mathcal{C}^0\).
487	\end{definition}
488	
489	\begin{definition}\leanok
490	[Relative {\v C}ech complex from a nerve]
491	  \label{def:relative_cech_complex_of_nerve}
492	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.relativeCechComplexOfNerve}
493	  \uses{def:cech_nerve}
494	  Given \(f : X \to S\) and an augmented cosimplicial \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module
495	  \(N\), the \emph{relative {\v C}ech cochain complex} in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)
496	  obtained by forgetting the augmentation of \(N\), pushing the resulting
497	  cosimplicial object forward degreewise along \(f\), and taking the
498	  alternating-coface-map cochain complex. This passage uses only the preadditivity
499	  of \(\operatorname{QCoh}(S)\) and \emph{no} pushforward/pullback coherence; it is
500	  the coherence-free plumbing (3) that turns the nerve of
501	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} into the {\v C}ech complex.
502	\end{definition}
503	
504	\section{Affine acyclicity of the {\v C}ech complex}
505	
506	\begin{definition}
507	[Standard affine cover from a spanning family]
508	  \label{def:standard_affine_cover}
509	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}
510	  \mathlibok
511	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Cover.Open}).}
512	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring (so \(R = \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R,
513	  \mathcal{O})\)) and let \(s : \iota \to R\) be a family of elements that generate
514	  the unit ideal, \(\operatorname{Ideal.span}(\operatorname{range} s) = \top\). The
515	  \emph{standard affine open cover} of \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) associated with
516	  \(s\) is the affine open cover whose index set is \(\iota\), whose \(i\)-th member
517	  is the basic open \(D(s_i)\), and whose \(i\)-th structure morphism is the map of
518	  affine schemes induced by the localisation homomorphism \(R \to R_{s_i}\); that
519	  is, in Mathlib notation the defining equation
520	  \(\bigl(\text{affineOpenCoverOfSpanRangeEqTop}\bigr)_f\, i =
521	  \operatorname{Spec.map}\bigl(\text{algebraMap}\; R\; (\operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i))\bigr)\),
522	  so that the sections over the \(i\)-th piece are the \emph{away} localisation
523	  \(R_{s_i} = \operatorname{Localization.Away}(s_i)\) and the geometric piece is
524	  \(D(s_i)\). This is exactly the standard open covering
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R = \bigcup_i D(s_i)\) by basic opens, packaged as a single
526	  cover object. It is the spanning-family bundle \((s, hs)\) that the affine
527	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is stated over.
528	\end{definition}
529	
530	\subsection{The categorical--module bridge (L1)}
531	\label{sec:cech_l1_bridge}
532	
533	The affine vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine} is a statement about the
534	\emph{section} {\v C}ech complex \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U},
535	\mathcal{F})\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_complex} --- a cochain complex of
536	abelian groups --- whereas the combinatorial contracting-homotopy core operates on a
537	concrete complex of localised \(R\)-modules. The passage between the two, the
538	\emph{categorical--module bridge}, decomposes into two self-contained statements: the
539	section identification (each section group is an away localisation, with the
540	restriction maps the canonical localisation maps) and the homology-to-exactness
541	reduction (vanishing of the abstract homology is equivalent to exactness of the
542	underlying localised-module complex). These are isolated as
543	Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized} and
544	Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact} below; the assembly --- feeding each
545	positive-degree node through the local-to-global exactness criterion and closing with
546	the dependent combinatorial port --- is carried out in the proof of
547	Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
548	
549	\begin{definition}
550	
551	[Quasi-coherent sections over a basic open are an away localisation]
552	  \label{def:qcoh_sections_localized}

576	  \(\mathcal{O}_{\operatorname{Spec} R}\)-module, and write

705	    \;\Longrightarrow\;

752	  \uses{def:qcoh_sections_localized, def:section_cech_complex}
753	  The term identification is the degreewise statement of
754	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: the degree-\(p\) section group
755	  \(\prod_\sigma \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is \(\prod_\sigma M_{s_\sigma}\), since
756	  each \((p+1)\)-fold intersection \(D(s_{\sigma(0)}) \cap \cdots \cap
757	  D(s_{\sigma(p)})\) is the basic open \(D(s_\sigma)\) of the product
758	  \(s_\sigma = \prod_k s_{\sigma(k)}\) and the sections there are the away
759	  localisation \(M_{s_\sigma}\). The differential identification is item~(5) of
760	  Definition~\ref{def:qcoh_sections_localized}: each face restriction
761	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(s_{\sigma \circ d_j})) \to \mathcal{F}(D(s_\sigma))\) is the
762	  canonical localisation map \(M_{s_{\sigma \circ d_j}} \to M_{s_\sigma}\), so the
763	  alternating sum of restrictions is the alternating sum of localisation maps, the
764	  differential of \(D^\bullet\) (the coface match of
765	  Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_coface_match}). The homology vanishing then follows in
766	  three steps. First, \(\operatorname{exactAt\_iff\_isZero\_homology}\) turns
767	  \(\operatorname{IsZero}\) of the homology at \(p\) into \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) of
768	  the short complex at \(p\). Second, because the degree-\(p\) objects are
769	  \emph{categorical products in \(\mathrm{Ab}\)} rather than \(R\)-modules, the abelian
770	  analogue \(\operatorname{ShortComplex.ab\_exact\_iff}\)
771	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_ab_exact}) --- not the module-category criterion ---
772	  reduces \(\operatorname{ExactAt}\) to the underlying-group exactness
773	  \(\forall x_2,\; g(x_2) = 0 \Rightarrow \exists x_1,\; f(x_1) = x_2\). Third, the
774	  element-level product equivalence
775	  \(\operatorname{ToType}(\prod^{c} F_\sigma) \simeq \prod_\sigma
776	  \operatorname{ToType}(F_\sigma)\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_product_equiv}
777	  identifies each product group with the per-\(\sigma\) localised module \(D^p\), so the
778	  group-level exactness is exactly \(\operatorname{Function.Exact}(D^{p-1} \to D^p \to
779	  D^{p+1})\), supplied by \(\operatorname{dDiff\_exact}\)
780	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}).

782	  The identification of the abstract section complex with the concrete localised
783	  complex \(D^\bullet\) --- together with the construction of the prepend transport
784	  \(c\) and the localisation cofaces \(\delta_k\) discharging the unit, shift, and
785	  coface-commutation compatibilities \(\mathtt{hu}\)/\(\mathtt{hsh}\)/\(\mathtt{hcomm}\)

790	  ``\(\Gamma\) preserves homology'', which would presuppose the very affine exactness
791	  being proved.
792	\end{proof}

6123	  \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G}) = 0\).
6124	\end{lemma}
6125	\begin{proof}
6126	  \uses{def:section_cech_functoriality}
6127	  The section {\v C}ech complex is functorial in the coefficient presheaf
6128	  (Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_functoriality}), so \(e\) induces an isomorphism of section
6129	  {\v C}ech complexes \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong
6130	  \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G})\), and applying the degree-\(p\) homology
6131	  functor yields an isomorphism \(\check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \cong
6132	  \check{H}^p(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{G})\). A group isomorphic to a zero object is itself zero.
6133	\end{proof}

6145	\begin{lemma}[A standard cover of \(D(f)\) spans the unit ideal of \(R_f\)]
6146	  \label{lem:affine_cover_span_localizationAway}
6147	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.affine_cover_span_localizationAway}
6148	  \uses{lem:iSup_basicOpen_eq_top_iff_span}
6149	  \textit{Project-local infrastructure.}
6150	  Let \(R\) be a ring, \(f \in R\), and \(g : \iota \to R\) a finite family with \(D(f) =
6151	  \bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec} R\). Then the images
6152	  \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (g_i)\) span the unit ideal of \(R_f =
6153	  \operatorname{Localization.Away} f\):
6154	  \[
6155	    \operatorname{span}\bigl(\{\, g_i/1 : i \in \iota \,\}\bigr) = R_f.
6156	  \]
6157	\end{lemma}
6158	\begin{proof}
6159	  Pull the equality \(D(f) = \bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) back along the canonical open immersion
6160	  \(\operatorname{Spec} R_f \cong D(f) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{Spec} R\), whose underlying
6161	  continuous map is the comap of \(\operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\). Comap commutes with
6162	  distinguished opens and with suprema, so the pullback of \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\) is
6163	  \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i/1)\), while the pullback of \(D(f)\) is all of \(\operatorname{Spec} R_f\)
6164	  because \(f\) is a unit in \(R_f\). Thus \(\bigsqcup_i D(g_i/1) = \operatorname{Spec} R_f\), which
6165	  by the span criterion (a family of distinguished opens covers the whole spectrum iff the elements
6166	  generate the unit ideal) is equivalent to \(\operatorname{span}\{g_i/1\} = R_f\).
6167	\end{proof}

6226	  \uses{lem:section_cech_module_exact, lem:section_cech_homology_exact,
6227	    lem:affine_cover_span_localizationAway, lem:away_comparison_isLocalizedModule}
6228	  Set \(R_f = \operatorname{Localization.Away} f\) and \(M_f =
6229	  \operatorname{LocalizedModule}(\operatorname{Submonoid.powers} f)\, M\). Following the Stacks proof's
6230	  ``Write \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\)'' step, we run the whole computation over the ring \(R_f\),
6231	  where the cover \emph{does} span the unit ideal, and transport the resulting exactness back to \(R\)
6232	  algebraically.
6233	
6234	  \emph{The \(R_f\)-side complex is exact.} The un-localised section {\v C}ech module complex
6235	  \(D^\bullet\) of Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex} is polymorphic in the base ring:
6236	  for any commutative ring, any module over it, and any spanning family of the unit ideal, its
6237	  positive-degree exactness is Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact}. Instantiate that lemma over
6238	  \(R_f\) with the module \(M_f\) and the family \(g/1 : \iota \to R_f\),
6239	  \((g/1)_i = \operatorname{algebraMap} R\, R_f\, (g_i)\). Over \(R_f\) this family spans the unit
6240	  ideal (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_cover_span_localizationAway}, using \(D(f) = \bigsqcup_i D(g_i)\)), so
6241	  the spanning hypothesis of Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_module_exact} is met and the \(R_f\)-side
6242	  complex \(D^\bullet_{R_f}\) --- whose degree-\(p\) term is \(\prod_\sigma (M_f)_{(g/1)_\sigma}\) ---
6243	  is exact in positive degrees.
6244	
6245	  \emph{Degreewise transfer to the \(R\)-side.} For a tuple \(\sigma : \{0, \ldots, p\} \to \iota\) put
6246	  \(g_\sigma = \prod_k g_{\sigma k}\). Since each \(D(g_i) \subseteq D(f)\) we have \(D(g_\sigma)
6247	  \subseteq D(f)\), hence \(g_\sigma \in \sqrt{(f)}\) and \(f\) is invertible in the away localisation
6248	  \(M_{g_\sigma}\). The transitivity comparison of
6249	  Lemma~\ref{lem:away_comparison_isLocalizedModule} therefore exhibits a canonical isomorphism of
6250	  abelian groups
6251	  \[
6252	    M_{g_\sigma} \;\cong\; (M_f)_{g_\sigma},
6253	  \]
6254	  both sides presenting \(M\) localised away from \(g_\sigma f\). These per-tuple isomorphisms are the
6255	  vertical maps of a ladder between the \(R\)-side section {\v C}ech module complex of \(\widetilde{M}\)
6256	  over \(\{D(g_i)\}\) and the \(R_f\)-side complex \(D^\bullet_{R_f}\). They commute with the
6257	  alternating-sum localisation differentials, because each {\v C}ech coface is itself an
6258	  away-localisation comparison map and the comparisons of
6259	  Lemma~\ref{lem:away_comparison_isLocalizedModule} are natural in the localisation base (the same
6260	  naturality square intertwining the complex differential with the section cofaces in
6261	  Definition~\ref{def:section_cech_module_complex}). Transporting positive-degree exactness across this
6262	  additive ladder --- an exactness transfer along an isomorphism of complexes --- yields positive-degree
6263	  exactness for the \(R\)-side section {\v C}ech differentials.
6264	
6265	  \emph{From exactness to vanishing homology.} Wrapping positive-degree function exactness as the
6266	  vanishing of the degree-\(p\) homology --- exactly as Lemma~\ref{lem:section_cech_homology_exact}
6267	  does for the whole-space case --- gives \(\check{H}^p(\{D(g_i)\}, \widetilde{M}) = 0\) for all
6268	  \(p > 0\).
6269	\end{proof}

6278	dimension-shift induction \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\) for \(p > 0\)

6280	chains these.

6282	\subsection{Section {\v C}ech infrastructure and the cover-system encoding}
6283	
6284	The sub-lemmas below are formalized in a \emph{cover-local}, presheaf-level, hypothesis-driven
6285	form: each lemma fixes a single index family \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) and works with the
6286	section {\v C}ech complexes of that one covering, taking the geometric inputs (section-level
6287	exactness on faces, positive-degree {\v C}ech vanishing) as hypotheses. The cover-global
6288	\((\mathcal{B}, \mathrm{Cov})\) data of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_to_cohomology_on_basis} re-enters only
6289	in the inductive step (Lemma~\ref{lem:absolute_cohomology_pos_vanishing}), where it is packaged by
6290	the cover-system encoding (Definition~\ref{def:basis_cov_system},
6291	Definition~\ref{def:has_vanishing_higher_cech}) and discharges those hypotheses via the per-face
6292	short-exact-sequence derivation (Lemma~\ref{lem:face_ses_of_sheaf_ses}). The supporting
6293	definitions and the degreewise product engine are collected first.

6968	\section{The {\v C}ech complex computes $R^i f_*$}
6969	
6970	The comparison is proved by the \emph{acyclic-resolution} (Cartan--Leray) route. The
6971	augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) whose terms are
6972	acyclic for \(f_*\); the abstract homological-algebra
6973	Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then identifies the derived
6974	functor with the cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\). The two
6975	geometric inputs are isolated in the following sub-lemmas.

6977	\begin{lemma}
6978	[The augmented {\v C}ech complex is a resolution]
6979	  \label{lem:cech_augmented_resolution}
6980	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.cechAugmented_exact}

6984	  % extended {\v C}ech complex) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L68--82).
6985	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "We are asked to show that the extended complex
6986	  % \begin{equation}
6987	  % \label{equation-extended}
6988	  % 0 \to
6989	  % M \to
6990	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \to
6991	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}} \to
6992	  % \prod\nolimits_{i_0i_1i_2} M_{f_{i_0}f_{i_1}f_{i_2}} \to
6993	  % \ldots
6994	  % \end{equation}
6995	  % (whose truncation we have studied in
6996	  % Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-cover-module}) is exact.

7000	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7001	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
7002	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and let
7003	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover with all
7004	  intersections affine. Form the augmented {\v C}ech complex on \(X\),
7005	  \[
7006	    0 \;\to\; \mathcal{F} \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^0 \;\to\; \mathcal{C}^1 \;\to\;
7007	    \mathcal{C}^2 \;\to\; \cdots,
7008	    \qquad
7009	    \mathcal{C}^p \;=\; \prod_{(i_0, \ldots, i_p) \in I^{p+1}}
7010	      (j_{i_0 \ldots i_p})_*\bigl(\mathcal{F}|_{U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}}\bigr),
7011	  \]
7012	  the degree-\(p\) term being the degree-\(p\) object of the {\v C}ech nerve of
7013	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}, with augmentation the canonical map
7014	  \(\mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0\). This augmented complex is exact; that is, the
7015	  {\v C}ech nerve is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
7016	\end{lemma}
7017	
7018	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "It suffices to show that (\ref{equation-extended})
7019	% is exact after localizing at a prime $\mathfrak p$, see
7020	% Algebra, Lemma \ref{algebra-lemma-characterize-zero-local}.
7021	% In fact we will show that the extended complex localized
7022	% at $\mathfrak p$ is homotopic to zero.
7023	% There exists an index $i$ such that $f_i \not \in \mathfrak p$.
7024	% Choose and fix such an element $i_{\text{fix}}$. Note that
7025	% $M_{f_{i_{\text{fix}}}, \mathfrak p} = M_{\mathfrak p}$. ...
7026	% Let us define a homotopy ... by the rule
7027	% $h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} = s_{i_{\text{fix}} i_0 \ldots i_p}$"
7028	% (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L80--110).
7029	\begin{proof}
7030	  \uses{def:cech_nerve, lem:cech_acyclic_affine, lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections}
7031	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes,
7032	  \texttt{lemma-cech-cohomology-quasi-coherent-trivial} (proof).}
7033	  Exactness of a complex of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules is a \emph{local}
7034	  question: the complex is exact if and only if it is exact on every stalk
7035	  (equivalently, after localizing the sections at every prime). It therefore
7036	  suffices to check exactness of the augmented {\v C}ech complex stalkwise.
7037	
7038	  Fix an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) of \(X\) and a prime
7039	  \(\mathfrak{p} \in \operatorname{Spec}(A)\). By the affine tilde isomorphism
7040	  (Lemmas~\ref{lem:qcoh_iso_tilde_sections} and \ref{lem:qcoh_isIso_fromTildeGamma}) we have
7041	  \(\mathcal{F}|_U \cong \widetilde{M}\) with \(M = \Gamma(U, \mathcal{F})\), and the
7042	  sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over the faces are the away-localizations
7043	  \(M_{g_\sigma}\). The localization at \(\mathfrak{p}\) of the augmented {\v C}ech
7044	  complex over \(U\) is thus the localization at \(\mathfrak{p}\) of the extended
7045	  complex of localizations
7046	  \[
7047	    0 \;\to\; M \;\to\; \prod_{i_0} M_{f_{i_0}} \;\to\;
7048	      \prod_{i_0 i_1} M_{f_{i_0} f_{i_1}} \;\to\; \cdots.
7049	  \]
7050	  Since the \(\{f_i\}\) cover \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), for the prime
7051	  \(\mathfrak{p}\) there is an index \(i\) with \(f_i \notin \mathfrak{p}\); that is,
7052	  \(f_i\) becomes a \emph{unit} in \(A_{\mathfrak{p}}\). Fixing one such index
7053	  \(i_{\mathrm{fix}}\), the standard ``one index is a unit'' contracting homotopy
7054	  \[
7055	    h(s)_{i_0 \ldots i_p} \;=\; s_{i_{\mathrm{fix}}\, i_0 \ldots i_p}
7056	  \]
7057	  witnesses that the localized extended complex is homotopic to zero, hence exact in
7058	  every positive degree and at the augmentation. This is precisely the standard-cover
7059	  {\v C}ech vanishing already established at the level of sections in
7060	  Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}.
7061	
7062	  As this holds for every prime \(\mathfrak{p}\) — hence on every stalk — the
7063	  augmented {\v C}ech complex is exact on \(X\); that is, the {\v C}ech nerve of
7064	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve} is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
7065	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
7066	\end{proof}

7068	\begin{lemma}
7069	[Cohomology sheaf is the sheafification of the objectwise homology]
7070	  \label{def:cohomology_sheaf_is_sheafify_homology}
7071	  \lean{PresheafOfModules.homologyIsoSheafify,
7072	    PresheafOfModules.counitComplexIso,
7073	    PresheafOfModules.sheafificationAdditive,
7074	    CategoryTheory.Functor.mapHomologyIso'}
7075	  % NOTE: project-built generalization. Stacks Tag~01XJ
7076	  % (\texttt{lemma-describe-higher-direct-images}) establishes the consequence
7077	  % $R^if_*\mathcal{F} \cong \text{sheafification of }(V \mapsto H^i(f^{-1}(V),\mathcal{F}))$
7078	  % but does not state sheafification commuting with the homology of an arbitrary
7079	  % cochain complex as a standalone lemma. No verbatim Stacks source quote for this block.
7080	  Let \(K\) be a cochain complex of presheaves of modules over a site admitting
7081	  sheafification. For every index \(i\) there is a natural
7082	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th homology of the sheafified complex and the
7083	  sheafification of the \(i\)-th objectwise (presheaf-level) homology of \(K\),
7084	  \[
7085	    H^i\bigl(\operatorname{sheafify}(K)\bigr)
7086	      \;\cong\;
7087	    \operatorname{sheafify}\bigl(H^i(K)\bigr).
7088	  \]
7089	  Equivalently, sheafification commutes with the formation of homology. This is the
7090	  reusable engine underlying the presheaf description of the higher direct images
7091	  (Stacks Tag~01XJ): it is what turns the resolution-internal cohomology sheaf into a
7092	  sheafification of an explicit cohomology presheaf.
7093	\end{lemma}
7094	
7095	\begin{proof}
7096	  Sheafification is exact: as the left adjoint of the inclusion of sheaves into
7097	  presheaves it preserves all colimits, and on a site admitting sheafification it
7098	  additionally preserves finite limits, so it preserves both the kernels and the
7099	  cokernels through which homology is computed. An exact additive functor commutes with
7100	  the homology functor of a cochain complex; chasing this through the counit complex
7101	  isomorphism \(\operatorname{sheafify}(\iota K) \cong K\) (the inclusion–sheafification
7102	  adjunction counit, an isomorphism on sheaves) gives
7103	  \(H^i(K) \cong H^i(\operatorname{sheafify} K) \cong
7104	  \operatorname{sheafify}(H^i(K))\). The supporting bricks are the
7105	  additivity of sheafification, the counit complex isomorphism, and the
7106	  complex-level transport of the short-complex map–homology isomorphism along an
7107	  additive homology-preserving functor.
7108	\end{proof}

7110	\begin{lemma}
7111	[Presheaf description of the higher direct images]
7112	  \label{lem:higher_direct_image_presheaf}
7113	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.higherDirectImage_iso_sheafify_presheafHomology,
7114	    AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardResolutionPresheafComplex}
